orig: ἡνίκα ἡτοίμαζεν τὸν οὐρανόν συμπαρήμην αὐτῷ καὶ ὅτε ἀφώριζεν τὸν ἑαυτοῦ θρόνον ἐπ’ ἀνέμων · καὶ ἡνίκα ἰσχυρὰ ἐποίει τὰ ἄνω νέφη καὶ ὡς ἀσφαλεῖς ἐτίθει πηγὰς τῆς ὑπ’ οὐρανόν · καὶ ὡς ἰσχυρὰ ἐποίει τὰ θεμέλια τῆς γῆς · ἤμην παρ’ αὐτῷ ἁρμόζουσα · ἐγὼ ἤμην ἧ προσέχαιρεν καθ’ ἡμέραν δὲ εὐφραινόμην ἐν προσώπῳ αὐτοῦ ἐν παντὶ καιρῷ · ὅτε εὐφραίνετο τὴν οἰκουμένην συντελέσας καὶ εὐφραίνετο ἐν υἱοῖς ἀνθρώπων ·
title: Proverbs 8:22-31: Wisdom Before Creation
author: Solomon (attributed)

en: I have seen the task that God has given to the sons of men to be occupied with. He has made everything beautiful in its time. Also, he has placed eternity (aion, αἰών) in their hearts, yet so that man cannot find out the work that God has done from the beginning to the end.
orig: εἶδον σὺν τὸν περισπασμόν ὃν ἔδωκεν ὁ θεὸς τοῖς υἱοῖς τῶν ἀνθρώπων τοῦ περισπᾶσθαι ἐν αὐτῷ · σὺν τὰ πάντα ἐποίησεν καλὰ ἐν καιρῷ αὐτοῦ · καί γε σὺν τὸν αἰῶνα ἔδωκεν ἐν καρδίᾳ αὐτῶν · ὅπως μὴ εὕρῃ ὁ ἄνθρωπος τὸ ποίημα ὃ ἐποίησεν ὁ θεὸς ἀπ’ ἀρχῆς καὶ μέχρι τέλους ·
title: Ecclesiastes 3:10-11: Eternity in the Heart
author: Solomon (attributed)

en: The Word of God became man so that you too may learn from a man how a man becomes God.
orig: Λόγος ο του Θεου άνθρωπος γενόμενος, ίνα δη και συ παρα ανθρώπου μάθης πή ποτε άνθρωπος γένηται Θεός.
title: Protrepticus 1.8.4: The Word Became Man So You May Learn How Man Becomes God
author: Clement of Alexandria

en: The end is always like the beginning. As there is one end of all things, so we must understand that there is one beginning of all things, and as there is one end of many things, so from one beginning arise many differences and varieties, which in their turn are restored, through God’s goodness, through their subjection to Christ and their unity with the Holy Spirit, to one end which is like the beginning. That is to say, all those who bend the knee to the name of Jesus and thereby declare their subjection to him shall, in the restitution of all things, be gathered into one end. And God shall be all in all.
orig: Τέλος αεί τη αρχή όμοιον. Ώσπερ ουν εν πάντων τέλος, ουτως μίαν αρχήν πάντων νοείν δεί· και ως ενός τέλους πολλών όντος, ουτως απο μιας αρχής πολλαί διαφοραί και ποικιλίαι, αι πάλιν δια της χρηστότητος του Θεου αποκαθίστανται εις εν τέλος όμοιον τη αρχή· ίνα η ο Θεός τα πάντα εν πασιν.
title: De Principiis III.6.1: The End Is Like the Beginning
author: Origen of Alexandria

en: Through the Holy Spirit comes our restoration to Paradise, our ascent to the Kingdom of heaven, our adoption as sons, our freedom to call God our Father, our becoming partakers of the grace of Christ, our being called children of light, our sharing in eternal glory, and, in a word, our being brought into a state of all fullness of blessing, both in this world and in the world to come. Contemplating as in a mirror the blessings that are in store, which are promised to us through faith, we await the enjoyment of them as though they were already present, so extraordinary is the pleasure we take in them. If this is the pledge, what must the perfection be? And if the first-fruits are so great, what must the full harvest be? Through the Spirit we become like God; through the Spirit, what is beyond all desire: we become God.
orig: Δια του αγίου Πνεύματος η εις παράδεισον αποκατάστασις, η εις βασιλείαν ουρανών ανάβασις, η εις υιοθεσίαν επάνοδος, η παρρησία του καλείν Πατέρα τον Θεόν, το κοινωνον γενέσθαι της χάριτος του Χριστου, το τέκνον φωτος χρηματίζειν, το αιδίου δόξης μετέχειν· δια του Πνεύματος η προς Θεον ομοίωσις, και το υπερ πασαν επιθυμίαν, Θεον γενέσθαι.
title: On the Holy Spirit 9.23: Through the Spirit We Become God
author: Basil of Caesarea

en: He was mortal, but God. He was of the line of David, but the maker of Adam. He was clothed in flesh, but free from the things of the flesh. He had a mother, but a virgin mother. He was bound, yet he loosed. He was sold, and very cheaply — for thirty pieces of silver — yet he redeemed the world, and at great cost, for the price was his own blood. As a sheep he was led to the slaughter, but he is the Shepherd of Israel and now of the whole world. As a lamb he was silent, yet he is the Word, proclaimed by the voice of one crying in the wilderness. He was bruised and wounded, but he heals every disease and every infirmity. He was lifted up and nailed to the tree, but by the tree of life he restores us. He died, but he gives life, and by his death destroys death. He was buried, but he rose again. He descended into Hades, but he brought up souls from there. He ascended to heaven, and shall come again to judge the living and the dead. If the one set of facts makes you stumble, the other set should lift you up.
orig: Θνητός, αλλα Θεός· εκ γένους Δαβίδ, αλλα πλάστης Αδάμ· σαρκοφόρος, αλλα και ασώματος· μητρί μεν εγνωσθη, παρθένω δέ· εδέθη, αλλα και έλυσεν· επράθη, και λίαν ευτελώς, τριάκοντα αργυρίων· αλλα λυτρουται κόσμον, και μεγάλης τιμής, του ιδίου αίματος.
title: Third Theological Oration 19: On the Son and Theosis
author: Gregory of Nazianzus

en: The Godhead is purity, freedom from passion, and separation from all evil. If therefore these things are in you, God is indeed in you. If your mind is untouched by any evil, free from passion, purified from all stain, then you are blessed because of your clear-sightedness. For by looking at your own purity, you perceive the image of the divine nature as in a mirror and a reflection. And if we may use a somewhat bold expression, it is the nature of your mind that is the image of the beauty above. Just as in the case of mirrors, what appears in the material substance takes its form from the face that presents itself, so the nature that was formed after the divine is made like it, and if it looks upon the Good it is stamped with the image of the Good, while if it turns to the evil it takes upon itself the form of that which is contrary to the Good.
orig: Καθαρότης, απάθεια, κακου παντός αλλοτρίωσις η θεότης εστίν. Ει ουν ταυτα εν σοί, Θεός εν σοι πάντως εστίν. Ει ακήρατος κακου παντος η διάνοια, πάθους ελευθέρα, παντός μολυσμου καθαρεύουσα, μακάριος ει της οξυδερκίας· ότι το τοις κεκαθαρμένοις αθέατον, οιον εν κατόπτρω και εικόνι, της θείας φύσεως την εικόνα εν τη ιδία καθαρότητι εποπτεύων.
title: On the Making of Man 16: The Mirror of the Divine
author: Gregory of Nyssa

en: The human person was introduced last among beings, as a kind of natural bond mediating between the universal extremes through its own parts, and bringing into unity in itself those things that are by nature far distant from each other. Accordingly, the human person was to unite: first, the division of its own nature, as male and female, through a dispassionate relation to divine virtue, for which the distinctions of this division are in no way necessary; then, through the holiness of its life, it was to unite paradise and the inhabited earth, making of them one earth; then, by a life identical with that of the angels, it was to unite heaven and earth, making of them one creation; then, by a knowledge equal to that of the angels, it was to unite the sensible and the intelligible, making of them one world; and finally, through love, it was to unite created nature with the uncreated, showing them to be one and the same through the acquisition of grace — the whole human person wholly pervading the whole God, and becoming everything that God is, save at the level of identity of essence, and receiving to itself the whole of God.
orig: Τελευταιον των οντων εισήχθη ο ανθρωπος, οιον τις φυσικός σύνδεσμος δια των οικείων μερων πασι τοις ακροις μεσιτεύων, και εις εν αγων εν εαυτω τα πολλη τη φύσει διεστηκότα και αλλήλων απέχοντα· όλον όλω τω Θεω περιχωρήσαντα, και παν ο εστιν ο Θεός γενόμενον, χωρις της κατ’ ουσίαν ταυτότητος, και όλον εαυτω τον Θεον αντιλαβόν.
title: Ambigua 7: The Five Cosmic Divisions
author: Maximus the Confessor

en: For we are called to participate in the divine nature, as the wise Peter says, not metaphorically but truly, because the Holy Spirit plants in us a participation in himself and through himself in the divine nature. For we have been made partakers of Christ and of the Holy Spirit, and the beauty of the ineffable Godhead shines forth in our souls through the Spirit who dwells in us. And thus those who are worthy to participate in the Spirit are called gods, not by grace alone, as those who are promoted to some honour, but because they truly have God dwelling in them and with them.
orig: Κεκλήμεθα γαρ εις μετουσίαν της θείας φύσεως, καθά φησιν ο σοφός Πέτρος, ου τροπικως αλλα αληθως· ότι το αγιον Πνευμα μετουσίαν εαυτου και δι εαυτου της θείας φύσεως εγκατασπείρει ημιν· μέτοχοι γαρ Θεου και του αγίου Πνεύματος γεγόναμεν.
title: Commentary on John 1:9: Participation in the Divine Nature
author: Cyril of Alexandria

en: The flesh was deified and made God and made equal in divinity with the Word by the union with him, without either nature undergoing any change. And the flesh of the Lord was enriched with divine operations on account of its most pure union with the Word, that is, by a hypostatic union, without any loss of its natural properties. For it is not by its own operation that the flesh acts divinely, but by the Word united with it, since the Word manifests its own operation through the flesh. For a red-hot iron burns, not because it has acquired the power of burning by some process of reasoning, but because it has come to possess this power through its union with the fire. The flesh, then, was mortal in itself but life-giving through its hypostatic union with the Word. And we say in like manner that the deification of the will was not a change in its natural character but a union with the Word — its own natural will being fulfilled in his divine and omnipotent will — so that the human nature has all the riches of the divine nature.
orig: Εθεώθη η σαρξ και Θεός εγένετο και ομόθεος τω Λόγω τη προς αυτον ενώσει, ουδετέρας των φύσεων τροπήν υπομεινάσης· καθάπερ ο πεπυρωμένος σίδηρος καίει, ουχ ότι λογισμω τινι το καίειν κεκτηται, αλλα τη προς το πυρ ενώσει τουτο κεκτημένος.
title: Exposition of the Orthodox Faith III.15: The Final Synthesis
author: John of Damascus

en: Theosis is the attaining of likeness to God and union with God, so far as is possible. This is the common goal of every hierarchy: the continuous love of God and of things divine, a love that is sacredly worked out in a divine and unified manner; and before this, the complete and irrevocable turning away from what is contrary, the knowledge of beings as they truly are, the vision and understanding of sacred truth, the inspired participation in the uniform perfection and in the One himself, so far as may be, the feast of contemplation which nourishes the intellect and deifies everyone who is raised up to it.
orig: Θέωσις εστιν η προς Θεον ως εφικτον αφομοίωσίς τε και ένωσις. Κοινός δε πάσης ιεραρχίας σκοπός η προς Θεόν τε και τα θεια συνεχής αγάπη, ενθέως τε και ενιαίως ιερουργουμένη.
title: Divine Names 2.11: Theosis as Union with God
author: Pseudo-Dionysius the Areopagite

en: God became man so that man might become God. But lest you think this means something unworthy, he adds a clarification through the Apostle: you are gods, and all of you children of the Most High. He made us gods by adopting us, not by generating us from himself. For there is only one Son of God by nature, who for our sake became the Son of Man, so that we who were sons of men by nature might become sons of God through him by grace. For he, remaining immutable, took on our nature so that in it he might take us up to himself, and, holding fast his own divinity, became a partaker of our weakness, so that we, being changed for the better, might by participating in his righteousness and immortality lose our own sinfulness and mortality, and preserve whatever good nature he had planted in us, now perfected by the greater good of his divine gift.
orig: Factus est Deus homo, ut homo fieret Deus. Sed ne quid indignum intellegas, addit per apostolum: ego dixi, dii estis, et filii Excelsi omnes. Fecit nos deos adoptando, non de se generando. Unus enim est Filius Dei natura, qui propter nos factus est filius hominis, ut nos qui eramus filii hominum natura, per illum efficeremur filii Dei gratia. Ille enim incommutabilis manens, naturam nostram suscepit, ut in ea nos susciperet ad se; et tenens suam divinitatem, particeps factus est nostrae infirmitatis, ut nos in melius commutati, quod peccatum et mortalitas nostra est, eius iustitiae et immortalitatis participatione amitteremus, et quod boni in natura nostra ille plantaverat, bono maiore divini muneris perfectum servaremus.
title: Sermon 166.4: The Western Theosis with Qualifications
author: Augustine of Hippo

en: Jesus said: If those who lead you say to you, “See, the kingdom is in the sky,” then the birds of the sky will precede you. If they say to you, “It is in the sea,” then the fish will precede you. Rather, the kingdom is within you and outside you. When you come to know yourselves, then you will be known, and you will realize that you are the children of the living Father. But if you do not know yourselves, then you dwell in poverty, and you are that poverty.
orig: Ⲑⲁⲟⲉ ⲓⲉ · ⲣⲉⲓⲁⲓⲉⲥ ⲓⲉⲓ · λέγει Ἰησοῦς · ἐὰν οἱ ὡγούμενοι λέγωσιν ὑμῖν · ἤ βασιλεία ἐντὸς ὑμῶν ἐστιν καὶ ἐκτὸς ὑμῶν
title: Gospel of Thomas: The Kingdom Within (Sayings 3, 22, 50, 77, 108)
author: Unknown (Gospel of Thomas)

en: Jesus said to them: When you make the two one, and when you make the inside like the outside and the outside like the inside, and the above like the below, and when you make the male and the female one and the same, so that the male not be male nor the female female—then you will enter the kingdom.
orig: λέγει Ἰησοῦς · ὅταν ποιήσητε τὰ δύο ἕν · καὶ τὸ ἔσωθεν ὡς τὸ ἔξωθεν · καὶ τὸ ἄνω ὡς τὸ κάτω
title: Gospel of Thomas: The Kingdom Within (Sayings 3, 22, 50, 77, 108)
author: Unknown (Gospel of Thomas)

en: Jesus said: If they say to you, “Where did you come from?” say to them, “We came from the light, the place where the light came into being on its own accord and established itself and became manifest through their image.” If they say to you, “Is it you?” say, “We are its children, and we are the elect of the living Father.” If they ask you, “What is the sign of your Father in you?” say to them, “It is movement and repose.”
orig: λέγει Ἰησοῦς · ἐὰν λέγωσιν ὑμῖν · πόθεν ἐλήλυθατε · λέγετε αὐτοῖς · ἐκ τοῦ φωτὸς ἐλήλυθαμεν
title: Gospel of Thomas: The Kingdom Within (Sayings 3, 22, 50, 77, 108)
author: Unknown (Gospel of Thomas)

en: Jesus said: I am the light that is over all things. I am all. From me all came forth, and to me all attained. Split a piece of wood: I am there. Lift up the stone, and you will find me there.
orig: λέγει Ἰησοῦς · ἐγώ εἰμι τὸ φῶς τὸ ἐπί πᾶσιν · ἐγώ εἰμι τὸ πᾶν · σχίσον ξύλον · ἐγώ ἐκεῖ εἰμι
title: Gospel of Thomas: The Kingdom Within (Sayings 3, 22, 50, 77, 108)
author: Unknown (Gospel of Thomas)

en: Jesus said: He who will drink from my mouth will become like me. I myself shall become he, and the things that are hidden will be revealed to him.
orig: λέγει Ἰησοῦς · ὁ πίνων ἐκ τοῦ στόματός μου · γενήσεται ὡς ἐγώ · καὶ ἐγώ αὐτὸς γενήσομαι
title: Gospel of Thomas: The Kingdom Within (Sayings 3, 22, 50, 77, 108)
author: Unknown (Gospel of Thomas)

en: It is not possible for anyone to see anything of the things that actually exist unless he becomes like them. This is not the way with man in the world: he sees the sun without being a sun; and he sees the heaven and the earth and all other things, but he is not these things. This is quite in keeping with the truth. But you saw something of that place, and you became those things. You saw the Spirit, you became spirit. You saw Christ, you became Christ. You saw the Father, you shall become Father. So in this place you see everything and do not see yourself, but in that place you do see yourself—and what you see you shall become.
orig: οὐ δύνατόν ἐστιν οὐδένα ιἰδεῖν τι τῶν πραγμάτων τῶν ὑπαρχόντων · ἐὰν μὴ γένηται ὅμοιος αὐτοῖς · τὸ πνεῦμα εἶδες · πνεῦμα ἐγένου · τὸν Χριστὸν εἶδες · Χριστὸς ἐγένου
title: Gospel of Philip: You Saw Christ, You Became Christ
author: Valentinus

en: The chrism is superior to baptism, for it is from the word “chrism” that we have been called “Christians,” certainly not because of the word “baptism.” And it is because of the chrism that “the Christ” has his name. For the Father anointed the Son, and the Son anointed the apostles, and the apostles anointed us. He who has been anointed possesses everything. He possesses the resurrection, the light, the cross, the Holy Spirit. The Father gave him this in the bridal chamber; he merely accepted the gift.
orig: τὸ χρῖσμα ὑπερέχει τοῦ βαπτίσματος · ἐκ γὰρ τοῦ χρίσματος · Χριστιανοὶ ἐκλήθημεν
title: Gospel of Philip: You Saw Christ, You Became Christ
author: Valentinus

en:
The Monad is a monarchy with nothing above it. It is he who exists as God and Father of everything, the invisible one who is above everything, who exists as incorruption, dwelling in the pure light into which no eye can look. He is the invisible Spirit, of whom it is not right to think of him as a god, or something similar. For he is more than a god, since there is nothing above him, for no one lords it over him. He does not exist in something inferior to him, since everything exists in him. It is he who establishes himself. He is eternal, since he does not need anything. He is total perfection. A being can have a relationship with a being, but the Invisible Spirit is not among such things. He is at rest in silence, he who is before all things.
        And his thought (Ennoia) performed a deed, and she came forth—she who had appeared before him in the shine of his light. She is the first power, she who was before all of them, and who came forth from his mind: she is the forethought (Pronoia) of the All—her light shines like his light—the perfect power, the image of the invisible, virginal Spirit. She became the womb of everything, for it is she who is prior to them all: the Mother-Father, the first Human, the Holy Spirit, the triple-male, the triple-powered, the triple-named androgynous one. And the eternal aeon among the invisible ones: the first to come forth—Barbelo.
orig: τὸ ἀόρατον πνεῦμα · ἡ μονάς ἐστιν μοναρχία ἧς οὐδὲν ὑπεράνω ἐστιν · καὶ ἡ ἔννοια αὐτοῦ εἰργάσατο ἔργον · καὶ προῆλθεν · ἡ Βαρβηλώ
title: Apocryphon of John: The Barbelo Cosmogony
author: Unknown (Gospel of Thomas)

en:
And the power of the Mother went out of Yaltabaoth into the natural body that they had fashioned after the image of the one who exists from the beginning. The body moved and gained strength, and it was luminous. And in that moment the rest of the powers became jealous, because he had come into being through all of them—and they had given their power to the man—and his intelligence was greater than that of those who had made him, and greater than that of the chief archon. And when they recognized that he was luminous, and that he could think better than they, and that he was free from wickedness, they took him and threw him into the lowest region of all matter.
        But the blessed one, the Mother-Father, the beneficent and merciful one, had mercy on the power of the Mother that had been brought forth out of the chief archon. And he sent, through his beneficent Spirit and his great mercy, a helper to Adam—luminous Epinoia, she who is from him, who was called Life (Zoe). And she assists the whole creature, by toiling with him and restoring him to his fullness, and by teaching him about the descent of his seed, and teaching him about the way of ascent, which is the way he came down.
orig: καὶ ἡ δύναμις τῆς μητρὸς ἐξῆλθεν ἐκ τοῦ Ἰαλδαβαώθ · εἰς τὸ σῶμα τὸ φυσικόν · καὶ ἐκίνησεν καὶ ἔλαβεν ἰσχύν · καὶ ἐφώτισεν
title: Apocryphon of John: The Barbelo Cosmogony
author: Unknown (Gospel of Thomas)

s: Ignorance and the Origin of the World (16.31–17.36)
en: Since the deficiency came about because the Father was not known, therefore when the Father is known, from that moment on the deficiency will no longer exist. As in the case of a person’s ignorance—when he comes to have knowledge, his ignorance vanishes of itself; as darkness vanishes when light appears—so also the deficiency vanishes in the perfection. So from that moment on, the form is no longer apparent, but it will vanish in the fusion of unity, for now their works lie scattered. In time, unity will perfect the spaces. It is within unity that each one will attain himself; within knowledge he will purify himself from multiplicity into unity, consuming matter within himself like fire, and darkness by light, death by life.
orig: ἐπεὶ τὸ ὑστέρημα ἐγένετο ἐπεὶ ὁ πατὴρ οὐκ ἐγινώσκετο · ὅταν οὖν ὁ πατὴρ γινώσκηται · τὸ ὑστέρημα οὐκέτι ἔσται
title: Gospel of Truth: Knowledge as Return to the Father
author: Valentinus

s: Knowledge and Return to the Father (21.3–22.20)
en:
He discovered them in himself, and they discovered him in themselves—the incomprehensible, inconceivable one, the Father, the perfect one, the one who made all things. Within him are all things, and all things have need of him. Although he retained their perfection within himself, which he did not give to the all, the Father was not jealous. What jealousy indeed could there be between himself and his members? For if the aeon had thus received their perfection, they could not have come to the Father. He retains within himself their perfection, granting it to them as a return to him, and a knowledge that is uniquely his. It is he who fashioned the all, and the all is within him, and the all had need of him.
        As in the case of a person whom some do not know: he wishes them to know him and to love him—for what did the all have need of, if not the knowledge of the Father?—so he became a guide, restful and at leisure. In the middle of a school he came and spoke the word as a teacher. Those who were wise in their own estimation came to test him. But he confounded them, because they were foolish. They hated him because they were not truly wise.
orig: εὗρεν αὐτοὺς ἐν ἑαυτῷ · καὶ αὐτοὶ εὗρον αὐτὸν ἐν ἑαυτοῖς · τὸν ἀκατάληπτον · τὸν ἀνεννόητον · τὸν πατέρα · τὸν τέλειον
title: Gospel of Truth: Knowledge as Return to the Father
author: Valentinus

en:
The Father exists in the Undivided One. He is the one in whom the Totality dwells and whom the Totality lacks. He projected the Totalities, he who exists within himself, whom the Totality surrounds. It is the Father in whom the root of the Totality is established. He gave them their constitutions. He was not himself the one who brought himself forth. Each one of the Totalities is a thought, and a form, and a root.
        Silence is his companion. He it is who projected the Totality from himself—that is, the eternal ones—he projected them as roots and as a spring, as fruits. They are offspring of his thought and of his will. He is the one from whom all things proceed, the one whom they all lack, since he retained within himself what they needed, what he did not give to the Totalities—the knowledge of the Father. He is the one who brought the Totalities into existence, and they knew neither where they came from nor whither they were going.
orig: ὁ πατὴρ ὑπάρχει ἐν τῷ ἀμερίστῳ · αὐτός ἐστιν ἐν ἷ τὸ πλήρωμα κατοικεῖ · καὶ ὃν τὸ πλήρωμα ὑστερεῖ · ἡ σιγὴ σύνεστιν αὐτῷ
title: A Valentinian Exposition: The Pleroma and the Fall
author: Valentinus

en:
Sophia, who is called Achamoth, had conceived a desire without the consent of her consort. And since she had no partner, her product was imperfect—for it was fashioned without her consort. And it did not resemble its mother, for it was a different form. When she saw the consequence of her desire, it had changed into the form of a serpent and a lion—with eyes that flash with fire.
        She cast it away from herself, outside that place, so that none of the immortals might see it. For she had created it in ignorance. And she surrounded it with a luminous cloud and placed a throne in the middle of the cloud, so that no one might see it except the Holy Spirit, who is called the Mother of the Living. And she named it Yaltabaoth.
        But the restoration will come when the Totality reveals itself in the one who is the Son. That is the restoration: the return to what is from the beginning. The one who has knowledge is from above. He is called to what is above. The one who has heard is called to turn himself and to ascend to the Father and to receive the Totalities—the restoration—and to discover himself there, in the place of rest.
orig: ἡ Σοφία ἡ καλουμένη Ἀχαμώθ · ἐνεθυμήθη ἐπιθυμίαν ἄνευ τῆς συναινέσεως τοῦ συζύγου αὐτῆς · ἡ δὲ ἀποκατάστασις ἔσται · ὅταν τὸ πλήρωμα φανερωθῇ
title: A Valentinian Exposition: The Pleroma and the Fall
author: Valentinus

en:
In those days, in those far-off days, in those nights, in those far-off nights, in those years, in those far-off years—in those ancient days, when the things that were needful had been brought into being, in those ancient days, when the things that were needful had been given their proper nourishment—when bread had been baked in the shrines of the land, and bread had been tasted in the houses of the land—Shuruppak gave instructions to his son. Shuruppak, the son of Ubar-Tutu, gave instructions to his son Ziusudra:
        “My son, I would give you instruction. Take my instruction.”
orig:
ud ré-a ud sù-rá-ta
gi₆ ré-a gi₆ sù-rá-ta
mu ré-a mu sù-rá-ta
ud ul ré-a
ud ul-là nig̲-si-sá kí-en-gi-ra dù-a-ba
ud ul-là nig̲-si-sá kí-en-gi-ra šà-ga-ba
ninda é-kùr-ra-ka ba-ab-šèg₆-ğá
ninda é kalam-ma-ka ba-an-gú-ul-a
Šuruppak-ke₄ dumu-ni-ra na-rí-gá-àm₃
Šuruppak dumu Ubar-Tutu-ke₄
dumu-ni Ziú-sud-rà-ra na-rí-gá-àm₃
dumu-ğu₁₀ na mu-un-rí-gá na de₅-gá
title: Instructions of Shuruppak: The Oldest Wisdom Text
author: Anonymous (Sumerian)

en:
“Do not buy a donkey that brays. It will split your very yoke.
        Do not locate a field on a road. Do not place your house near a public square.
        Do not vouch for someone. That man will have a hold on you.
        Do not curse with powerful means. The curse will recoil upon you.
        Do not speak arrogantly to your mother. That causes hatred for you.
        Do not question the words of your mother and your personal god.
        The elder brother is indeed like a father. The elder sister is indeed like a mother. Listen to your elder brother. Listen to your elder sister.
        Do not pass judgment when you drink beer.
        You should not steal anything. You should not kill yourself with a knife.
        Do not laugh with or sit alone in a room with a girl who is married.”
orig:
anše gù-dé-a na-an-sa₁₀
šudul-zu ba-e-da-bé
        a-šà ḫé-é-a na-an-gá-gá
é-zu sig₄-ba na-an-dù-dù
        lu₂ na-an-gi-in
lu₂-bi zu-a bí-ib-gá-gá
        nam-tar-ra ka-aš na-an-šè-šè
nam-tar-re zu-a bí-ib-gi₄-gi₄
        ama-zu-ùr inim gal na-an-ga-àm₃-dùg₄
nig̲-hul-ğál-la mu-un-da-an-šè-šè
        šeš-gal ad-da-àm
nin₉-gal ama-àm
šeš-gal-zu-ùr na-de₅-gá
nin₉-gal-zu-ùr na-de₅-gá
        kaš nağ-a-ni inim na-an-dí-dí
title: Instructions of Shuruppak: The Oldest Wisdom Text
author: Anonymous (Sumerian)

en:
“The night is long. Think upon it.
        My son, you should heed my instructions. Ziusudra, you should heed my instructions. The instructions of an old man are precious. May Utu let you attain them.”
        These are the instructions of Shuruppak, the son of Ubar-Tutu.
orig:
gi₆ gíd-da šà-bi zu-àm
        dumu-ğu₁₀ na de₅-ga-àm na ḫé-àm
Ziú-sud-rà na de₅-ga-àm na ḫé-àm
inim um-ma-ke₄ nig̲ kú-zu-àm
Utu-ùr ḫé-em-tà-a-è
        na-rí Šuruppak dumu Ubar-Tutu-ka-kam
title: Instructions of Shuruppak: The Oldest Wisdom Text
author: Anonymous (Sumerian)

s: Tablet I, lines 1–20: The Primordial Beginning
en:
When on high the heavens had not yet been named, and below the earth had not yet been called by name—when primordial Apsu, their begetter, and Mummu-Tiamat, she who bore them all, were mingling their waters as one, when no marsh had formed and no island could be found, when no god at all had appeared, had not been named, no destinies decreed—then were the gods created within them.
        Lahmu and Lahamu emerged, were called by name. Before they had grown, before they had matured, Anshar and Kishar were created, surpassing them. They lived for many days, adding years to years. Anu was their firstborn son, the rival of his fathers. Anshar made his firstborn Anu his equal. And Anu begat Nudimmud in his likeness.
        Nudimmud it was who was the master of his fathers: broad of understanding, wise, mighty in strength, far mightier than his grandfather Anshar. He had no rival among the gods, his brethren.
orig:

title: Enuma Elish: Creation from Divine Blood
author: Anonymous (Babylonian)

s: Tablet VI, lines 1–38: Creation of Humanity from Divine Blood
en:
When Marduk heard the speech of the gods, his heart moved him to create artful things. He opened his mouth and spoke to Ea, conveying the plan he had conceived in his heart:
        “I will bind together blood and cause bones to be. I will establish a savage, ‘Man’ shall be his name. Truly, savage-man I will create. He shall be charged with the service of the gods, that they might be at rest.”
        Ea answered him, speaking a word to him, telling him a plan for the relief of the gods:
        “Let one of the brothers be given up. Let him perish that mankind may be fashioned. Let the great gods assemble and let the guilty one be given up, that they may be confirmed.”
        Marduk assembled the great gods. Graciously he gave instruction, issued a command. The gods paid heed to his utterance. The king addressed a word to the Anunnaki:
        “If your earlier declaration was true, now declare the truth on oath by me! Who was it who contrived the uprising and made Tiamat rebel, and joined battle? Let him who contrived the uprising be given up, that I may lay his punishment upon him. You shall dwell in peace.”
        The great gods answered him, the king of the gods of heaven and earth:
        “It was Qingu who contrived the uprising and made Tiamat rebel, and joined battle.”
        They bound him, holding him before Ea. They imposed the punishment on him and severed his blood-vessels. From his blood they created mankind. Ea imposed the service of the gods upon them and set the gods free.

en:
Words spoken by the Osiris N when he arrives at this Hall of Two Truths, that he may be purified of all the evil that he has done, and that he may behold the faces of the gods:
        “Hail to you, great god, lord of the Two Truths! I have come before you, my lord. I have been brought that I might see your beauty. I know you. I know the names of the forty-two gods who are with you in this Hall of Two Truths, who live on those who store up evil, who swallow their blood on that day of the reckoning of characters before Wennefer.
        Behold, I have come to you. I have brought you truth. I have driven out falsehood for you. I have not done evil against any person. I have not mistreated cattle. I have not committed wrong in the Place of Truth.”

author: Anonymous (Egyptian)

en:
“I have not done falsehood against any person.
I have not impoverished my associates.
I have not done wrong in the Place of Truth.
I have not learned that which is not.
I have not done any harmful thing.
I have not made the beginning of each day with demands in excess of what was due to me.
I have not brought my name forward for honors.
I have not deprived the orphan of his property.
I have not done what the gods detest.
I have not caused a servant to be mistreated by his master.
I have not caused anyone to suffer.
I have not caused anyone to go hungry.
I have not caused anyone to weep.
I have not killed.
I have not ordered anyone to kill.
I have not caused suffering to any person.
I have not diminished the offerings in the temples.
I have not debased the bread of the gods.
I have not taken the milk from the mouths of children.
I have not driven cattle from their pasture.
I have not snared the birds of the gods.
I have not dammed water in its season.
I have not built a dam against running water.
I have not quenched a fire in its time.
I have not neglected the days of meat-offerings.
I am pure. I am pure. I am pure. I am pure.”

s: The Prologue: Divine Mandate of Kingship
en:
When the supreme god Anu, king of the Anunnaki, and Enlil, lord of heaven and earth, who determines the destinies of the land, allotted the divine lordship over all peoples to Marduk, the firstborn son of Ea, made him great among the Igigi gods, called Babylon by its exalted name, made it supreme in the four quarters of the world, and established for him within it an everlasting kingship whose foundations are as firm as heaven and earth—
        at that time, Anu and Enlil named me, Hammurabi, the devout prince who fears the gods, to cause justice to prevail in the land, to destroy the wicked and the evil, that the strong should not oppress the weak, to rise like the sun over the black-headed people, and to light up the land.
        I am Hammurabi, the shepherd called by Enlil. I am he who heaps up abundance and plenty, who makes everything perfect for the city of Nippur. I am the devout provider of Ekur. I am the capable king who restored Eridu to its rightful place, who purified the rites of Eabzu.
        I am the storm that rages over the four quarters of the world, who magnifies the fame of Babylon, who gladdens the heart of Marduk his lord, who daily attends upon Esagila. I am the descendant of royalty, whom the god Sin created, who enriched the city of Ur, the pious one who brings abundance to Ekishnugal.
        I am the wise king, the obedient one, who heeds the words of Shamash. I am the mighty one who has laid the foundations of Sippar, who clothed with green the shrines of Aya, who made the temple of Ebabbar rise like a mountain in heaven.
        The great gods have called me. I am the shepherd who brings peace, whose sceptre is just. My benevolent shade is spread over my city. In my bosom I carry the peoples of Sumer and Akkad. Under my protection they have prospered. I govern them in peace. I shelter them in my wisdom.

title: Code of Hammurabi: The Divine Mandate

en:
Splendid you rise in the horizon of heaven, O living Aten, creator of life! When you have dawned in the eastern horizon, you fill every land with your beauty. You are beautiful, great, radiant, high above every land. Your rays embrace the lands to the limit of all that you have made.
        You are Re: you have reached their limits. You subdue them for your beloved son. Though you are far away, your rays are on earth. Though you are in the faces of men, your movements are unseen.
        When you set in the western horizon, the land is in darkness, in the manner of death. They sleep in their chambers, their heads wrapped up, and no eye sees another. All their goods that are under their heads could be stolen, and they would not know it. Every lion comes out of its den. All the serpents bite. Darkness is a shroud, and the earth is in stillness, for he who made them rests in his horizon.
        But when the earth brightens, and you rise in the horizon and shine as the Aten by day, you drive away the darkness and offer your rays. The Two Lands are in festivity. People awake and stand on their feet, for you have raised them up. Their bodies are washed. They take their garments. Their arms are raised in praise at your appearance. The entire land goes about its work.

title: Great Hymn to the Aten: The Earliest Monotheism
author: Akhenaten (attributed)

en:
How manifold are your works! They are hidden from the face of man, O sole god, like whom there is no other! You made the earth as you wished, you alone: all peoples, herds, and flocks. All that is on earth, that walks on legs, that rises up and flies on wings.
        The foreign countries—Syria and Kush—and the land of Egypt: you set every man in his place, you make their necessities. Each one has his food, and his lifetime is reckoned. Their tongues are separate in speech, and their natures likewise. Their skins are different, for you have distinguished the foreign peoples.
        You make the Nile in the underworld, and you bring it forth as you desire, to give life to the people, inasmuch as you made them for yourself, lord of all of them, who wearies himself on their behalf. O lord of every land, who rises for them, O Aten of the day, great in majesty!
        All distant foreign countries, you make their life also. You have placed a Nile in heaven, that it may descend for them and make floods upon the mountains, like the great green sea, to water their fields among their towns.
        How effective they are, your plans, O lord of eternity! The Nile in heaven, it is your gift to the foreign peoples and to the beasts of every desert that go upon their feet. But the true Nile comes from the underworld for Egypt.
        Your rays suckle every field. When you rise, they live and grow for you. You make the seasons to nourish all that you have made: winter to cool them, and the heat of summer so they may taste you.
        You are in my heart, and there is no other who knows you except your son Akhenaten. You have made him wise in your plans and in your power. The world came into being by your hand, as you made them. When you have risen, they live. When you set, they die. You yourself are lifetime. One lives by you.

title: Great Hymn to the Aten: The Earliest Monotheism
author: Akhenaten (attributed)

en: The divine light is not knowledge, nor does it belong to the category of knowledge, but it surpasses knowledge. It is not that which is known, but that by which we are transformed. For the light deifies, and deification is beyond knowledge.
orig: Τὸ θεῖον φῶς οὐ γνῶσίς ἐστιν, οὐδὲ εἰς τὸ γένος τῆς γνώσεως ἀνήκει, ἀλλ᾽ ὑπὲρ γνῶσιν.
title: Triads I.3.5: The Divine Light Surpasses Knowledge
author: Gregory Palamas

en: We are called to become gods, not by nature but by grace. For just as he became man by nature who was God, so he makes gods by grace those who were men.
orig: Καλούμεθα θεοὶ γενέσθαι, οὐ φύσει ἀλλὰ χάριτι.
title: Homily 14 on John: Gods by Grace
author: John Chrysostom

en: The true gnostic has already become god, being assimilated to God as far as is possible for human nature. For likeness to God is the end of contemplation, and it is through contemplation that the soul becomes divine.
orig: Ἀληθῶς γνωστικὸς θεὸς ἤδη γέγονεν, ἐξομοιωθεὶς τῷ θεῷ καθ᾽ ὅσον οἷόν τε τῇ ἀνθρωπίνῃ φύσει.
title: Stromata VII.10: The True Gnostic Has Become God
author: Clement of Alexandria

en: He gave us divinity, we gave him humanity. He lowered himself to our level that he might raise us to his. He took on our likeness that he might give us his own.
orig: Yahb lan alahuta, yahbnan leh barnashuta. Etmakkakh lwathan d-nrawmnan lwatheh.
title: Hymns on Faith 10: He Gave Us Divinity
author: Ephrem the Syrian

en: Recognize your dignity, O Christian, and having been made a partaker of the divine nature, do not return to your former baseness by degenerate conduct. Remember whose head and whose body you are a member of.
orig: Agnosce, o Christiane, dignitatem tuam, et divinae consors factus naturae, noli in veterem vilitatem degeneri conversatione redire. Memento cuius capitis et cuius corporis sis membrum.
title: Christmas Sermon 21.3: Recognize Your Dignity
author: Pope Leo the Great

en: For God made man neither immortal nor mortal, but capable of both. If man had inclined toward the things of immortality by keeping the commandment of God, he would have received immortality as a reward and would have become god. But if he turned to the things of death by disobeying God, he would himself be the cause of his own death.
orig: Οὔτε γὰρ θνητὸν ἐποίησεν αὐτὸν οὔτε ἀθάνατον, ἀλλὰ δεκτικὸν ἀμφοτέρων· εἰ γὰρ ἔρεψεν ἐπὶ τὰ τῆς ἀθανασίας, τηρήσας τὴν ἐντολὴν τοῦ θεοῦ, μισθὸν ἐκομίσατο παρ᾽ αὐτοῦ τὴν ἀθανασίαν καὶ θεὸς ἐγένετο.
title: To Autolycus II.27: Man Would Have Become God
author: Theophilus of Antioch

en: You shall be a companion of the Deity and a co-heir with Christ, no longer enslaved by desires or passions or wasted by disease. For you have become god. Whatever sufferings you endured as a man, God gave to you because you were a man; but whatever belongs to God, God has promised to bestow upon you, because you have been deified.
orig: Έσῃ σύνθρονος τῷ θεῷ καὶ συγκληρονόμος Χριστοῦ, οὐκέτι ἐπιθυμίαις ἢ πάθεσιν ἢ νόσοις δουλαγωγούμενος. Γέγονας γὰρ θεός.
title: Refutation X.34: Companion of the Deity
author: Hippolytus of Rome

en: They shall be called gods, being sons of the Most High, and each one shall stand individually and be judged and be made worthy to become gods, having the power to become children of the Most High.
orig: Κληθήσονται θεοί, υἱοὶ ὑψίστου ὄντες, καὶ ἕκαστος καθ᾽ ἑαυτὸν στήσεται καὶ κριθήσεται.
title: Dialogue with Trypho 124: Called Gods
author: Justin Martyr

en: Having been made in the image of God and the likeness, man was formed by the very hands of God—that is, by the Son and the Spirit—to whom he said: Let us make man in our image and likeness. And God formed man from the dust of the earth, and breathed into his face the breath of life, and man became a living soul.
orig: Factus in imaginem Dei et similitudinem, a manibus Dei formatus est, id est Filii et Spiritus, quibus dixit: Faciamus hominem ad imaginem et similitudinem nostram. Et finxit Deus hominem de limo terrae, et insufflavit in faciem eius flatum vitae, et factus est homo in animam vivam.
title: Against Marcion II.4: Image and Likeness
author: Tertullian

en: Man was made to be immortal and incorruptible. For God did not create death, nor does he delight in the destruction of the living. He made all things for incorruption, and the generative forces of the world are wholesome.
orig: ὄτι ὁ ἄνθρωπος ἐπ᾽ ἀφθαρσίᾳ καὶ ἀθανασίᾳ γεγένηται· θεὸς γὰρ θάνατον οὐκ ἐποίησεν, οὐδὲ τέρπεται ἐπ᾽ ἀπωλείᾳ ζώντων.
title: On the Resurrection I.6: Made for Immortality
author: Methodius of Olympus

en: God was born as man so that man might be born as God. For this is the mystery of the great and hidden counsel: that he who is God might become what man is, so that man might become what God is.
orig: Deus homo natus est, ut homo Deus nasceretur. Hoc est enim magni et arcani consilii sacramentum, ut qui Deus est fieret quod homo est, ut homo fieret quod Deus est.
title: De Trinitate IX.4: Born as God
author: Hilary of Poitiers

en: He assumed the imperfect to make it perfect. He took upon himself the form of a servant so that he might confer on the servant the freedom of the Lord. He descended that we might ascend; he took on our weakness that he might give us his strength.
orig: Suscepit imperfectum ut perficeret. Formam servi accepit ut servo libertatem Domini conferret. Descendit ut ascenderemus; infirmitatem nostram suscepit ut nobis suam virtutem donaret.
title: On the Incarnation V.35: The Imperfect Made Perfect
author: Ambrose of Milan

en: The pure in heart shall see God, and seeing is becoming. For the one who sees the Good becomes good, and the one who looks upon the True becomes true, and the one who beholds God becomes divine. Whoever sees God has obtained all that is counted among the things of blessedness.
orig: Οἱ καθαροὶ τῇ καρδίᾳ τὸν θεὸν ὄψονται, καὶ τὸ ἴδεῖν γενέσθαί ἐστιν· ὁ γὰρ ἰδὼν τὸ ἀγαθὸν ἀγαθὸς γίνεται.
title: On the Beatitudes 7: Seeing Is Becoming
author: Gregory of Nyssa

en: We become members of Christ—and Christ becomes our members. Christ becomes my hand, Christ becomes my foot, and I, wretched one, am the hand and foot of Christ. I move my hand, and my hand is wholly Christ, for God’s divinity is united to me inseparably.
orig: Μέλη Χριστοῦ γινόμεθα, καὶ μέλη γίνεται ημῶν ὁ Χριστός· Χριστὸς γίνεται χείρ μου, Χριστὸς γίνεται πούς μου.
title: Hymn 25: Members of Christ
author: Symeon the New Theologian

en: The only-begotten Son of God, wanting to make us sharers in his divinity, assumed our nature, so that having become man he might make men gods.
orig: Unigenitus Dei Filius, suae divinitatis volens nos esse participes, naturam nostram assumpsit, ut homines deos faceret factus homo.
title: Summa Theologiae III.1.2: Sharers in His Divinity
author: Thomas Aquinas

en: If you love me, you will keep my commandments.
orig: ἐὰν ἀγαπᾶτέ με τὰς ἐντολὰς τὰς ἐμὰς τηρήσατε ·
title: John 14:15-21: I Am in My Father, and You in Me
author: John (attributed)

en: And I will ask the Father, and he will give you another Advocate (parakletos, παράκλητος), that he may be with you forever—the Spirit of truth, whom the world cannot receive, because it neither sees him nor knows him. You know him, because he remains with you and will be in you.
orig: καὶ ἐγὼ ἐρωτήσω τὸν πατέρα καὶ ἄλλον παράκλητον δώσει ὑμῖν · ἵνα μεθʼ ὑμῶν εἰς τὸν αἰῶνα ᾖ · τὸ πνεῦμα τῆς ἀληθείας · ὃ τὸ κόσμος οὐ δύναται λαβεῖν · ὅτι οὐ θεωρεῖ αὐτὸ οὐδὲ γινώσκει αὐτό · ὑμεῖς γινώσκετε αὐτό · ὅτι παρʼ ὑμῖν μένει καὶ ἐν ὑμῖν ἔσται ·
title: John 14:15-21: I Am in My Father, and You in Me
author: John (attributed)

en: I will not leave you as orphans; I am coming to you. Yet a little while and the world will see me no longer, but you will see me. Because I live, you also will live.
orig: οὐκ ἀφήσω ὑμᾶς ὀρφανούς · ἔρχομαι πρὸς ὑμᾶς · ἔτι μικρὸν καὶ ὁ κόσμος με οὐκέτι θεωρεῖ · ὑμεῖς δὲ θεωρεῖτέ με · ὅτι ἐγὼ ζῶ καὶ ὑμεῖς ζήσετε ·
title: John 14:15-21: I Am in My Father, and You in Me
author: John (attributed)

en: In that day you will know that I am in my Father, and you in me, and I in you. The one who has my commandments and keeps them, that one is the one who loves me. And the one who loves me will be loved by my Father, and I will love him and will manifest myself to him.
orig: ἐν ἐκείνῃ τῇ ἡμέρᾳ γνώσεσθε ὑμεῖς ὅτι ἐγὼ ἐν τῷ πατρί μου · καὶ ὑμεῖς ἐν ἐμοί · κἀγὼ ἐν ὑμῖν · ὁ ἔχων τὰς ἐντολάς μου καὶ τηρῶν αὐτάς · ἐκεῖνός ἐστιν ὁ ἀγαπῶν με · ὁ δὲ ἀγαπῶν με ἀγαπηθήσεται ὑπὸ τοῦ πατρός μου · κἀγὼ ἀγαπήσω αὐτὸν καὶ ἐμφανίσω αὐτῷ ἐμαυτόν ·
title: John 14:15-21: I Am in My Father, and You in Me
author: John (attributed)

en: I am the true vine, and my Father is the vinedresser. Every branch in me that does not bear fruit he takes away, and every branch that bears fruit he prunes, so that it may bear more fruit.
orig: ἐγώ εἰμι ἡ ἄμπελος ἡ ἀληθινή · καὶ ὁ πατήρ μου ὁ γεωργός ἐστιν · πᾶν κλῆμα ἐν ἐμοὶ μὴ φέρον καρπόν · αἴρει αὐτό · καὶ πᾶν τὸ καρπὸν φέρον · καθαίρει αὐτό · ἵνα καρπὸν πλείονα φέρῃ ·
title: John 15:1-8: I Am the Vine, You Are the Branches
author: John (attributed)

en: Already you are clean because of the word that I have spoken to you. Abide in me, and I in you. As the branch cannot bear fruit by itself unless it abides in the vine, neither can you unless you abide in me.
orig: ἤδη ὑμεῖς καθαροί ἐστε διὰ τὸν λόγον ὃν λελάληκα ὑμῖν · μείνατε ἐν ἐμοί · κἀγὼ ἐν ὑμῖν · καθὼς τὸ κλῆμα οὐ δύναται καρπὸν φέρειν ἀφʼ ἑαυτοῦ ἐὰν μὴ μένῃ ἐν τῇ ἀμπέλῳ · οὕτως οὐδὲ ὑμεῖς ἐὰν μὴ ἐν ἐμοὶ μένητε ·
title: John 15:1-8: I Am the Vine, You Are the Branches
author: John (attributed)

en: I am the vine, you are the branches. The one who abides in me and I in him, this one bears much fruit, because apart from me you can do nothing. If anyone does not abide in me, he is thrown out like the branch and withers; and they gather them and throw them into the fire, and they are burned.
orig: ἐγώ εἰμι ἡ ἄμπελος · ὑμεῖς τὰ κλήματα · ὁ μένων ἐν ἐμοὶ κἀγὼ ἐν αὐτῷ · οὗτος φέρει καρπὸν πολύν · ὅτι χωρὶς ἐμοῦ οὐ δύνασθε ποιεῖν οὐδέν · ἐὰν μή τις μένῃ ἐν ἐμοί · ἐβλήθη ἔξω ὡς τὸ κλῆμα καὶ ἐξηράνθη · καὶ συνάγουσιν αὐτὰ καὶ εἰς τὸ πῦρ βάλλουσιν · καὶ καίεται ·
title: John 15:1-8: I Am the Vine, You Are the Branches
author: John (attributed)

en: If you abide in me, and my words abide in you, ask whatever you wish, and it will be done for you. By this my Father is glorified, that you bear much fruit and become my disciples.
orig: ἐὰν μείνητε ἐν ἐμοὶ καὶ τὰ ῥήματά μου ἐν ὑμῖν μείνῃ · ὃ ἐὰν θέλητε αἰτήσασθε · καὶ γενήσεται ὑμῖν · ἐν τούτῳ ἐδοξάσθη ὁ πατήρ μου · ἵνα καρπὸν πολὺν φέρητε καὶ γένησθε ἐμοὶ μαθηταί ·
title: John 15:1-8: I Am the Vine, You Are the Branches
author: John (attributed)

en: Or do you not know that all of us who were baptized into Christ Jesus were baptized into his death? Therefore we were buried with him through baptism into death, so that just as Christ was raised from the dead through the glory of the Father, so also we might walk in newness of life.
orig: ἢ ἀγνοεῖτε ὅτι ὅσοι ἐβαπτίσθημεν εἰς Χριστὸν Ἰησοῦν · εἰς τὸν θάνατον αὐτοῦ ἐβαπτίσθημεν · συνετάφημεν οὖν αὐτῷ διὰ τοῦ βαπτίσματος εἰς τὸν θάνατον · ἵνα ὥσπερ ἠγέρθη Χριστὸς ἐκ νεκρῶν διὰ τῆς δόξης τοῦ πατρός · οὕτως καὶ ἡμεῖς ἐν καινότητι ζωῆς περιπατήσωμεν ·
title: Romans 6:3-11: Baptized into His Death
author: Paul (attributed)

en: For if we have been united with him in the likeness of his death, we shall certainly also be united with him in the likeness of his resurrection. We know that our old self was crucified with him, so that the body of sin might be rendered powerless, so that we would no longer be enslaved to sin. For the one who has died has been set free from sin.
orig: εἰ γὰρ σύμφυτοι γεγόναμεν τῷ ὁμοιώματι τοῦ θανάτου αὐτοῦ · ἀλλὰ καὶ τῆς ἀναστάσεως ἐσόμεθα · τοῦτο γινώσκοντες ὅτι ὁ παλαιὸς ἡμῶν ἄνθρωπος συνεσταυρώθη · ἵνα καταργηθῇ τὸ σῶμα τῆς ἁμαρτίας · τοῦ μηκέτι δουλεύειν ἡμᾶς τῇ ἁμαρτίᾳ · ὁ γὰρ ἀποθανὼν δεδικαίωται ἀπὸ τῆς ἁμαρτίας ·
title: Romans 6:3-11: Baptized into His Death
author: Paul (attributed)

en: Now if we died with Christ, we believe that we shall also live with him, knowing that Christ, having been raised from the dead, dies no more; death no longer has dominion over him. For the death he died, he died to sin once for all; but the life he lives, he lives to God. So you also must consider yourselves dead to sin and alive to God in Christ Jesus.
orig: εἰ δὲ ἀπεθάνομεν σὺν Χριστῷ · πιστεύομεν ὅτι καὶ συνζήσομεν αὐτῷ · εἰδότες ὅτι Χριστὸς ἐγερθεὶς ἐκ νεκρῶν οὐκέτι ἀποθνήσκει · θάνατος αὐτοῦ οὐκέτι κυριεύει · ὃ γὰρ ἀπέθανεν · τῇ ἁμαρτίᾳ ἀπέθανεν ἐφάπαξ · ὃ δὲ ζῇ · ζῇ τῷ θεῷ · οὕτως καὶ ὑμεῖς λογίζεσθε ἑαυτοὺς νεκροὺς μὲν τῇ ἁμαρτίᾳ · ζῶντας δὲ τῷ θεῷ ἐν Χριστῷ Ἰησοῦ ·
title: Romans 6:3-11: Baptized into His Death
author: Paul (attributed)

en: And we all, with unveiled face, beholding as in a mirror the glory of the Lord, are being transformed (metamorphoumetha, μεταμορφούμεθα) into the same image, from glory to glory, just as from the Lord, the Spirit.
orig: ἡμεῖς δὲ πάντες ἀνακεκαλυμμένῳ προσώπῳ τὴν δόξαν κυρίου κατοπτριζόμενοι · τὴν αὐτὴν εἰκόνα μεταμορφούμεθα ἀπὸ δόξης εἰς δόξαν · καθάπερ ἀπὸ κυρίου πνεύματος ·
title: 2 Corinthians 3:18: Transformed from Glory to Glory
author: Paul (attributed)

en: Therefore, if anyone is in Christ—new creation (kainē ktisis, καινὴ κτίσις). The old things have passed away; behold, new things have come.
orig: ὥστε εἴ τις ἐν Χριστῷ · καινὴ κτίσις · τὰ ἀρχαῖα παρῆλθεν · ἰδοὺ γέγονεν καινά ·
title: 2 Corinthians 5:17-21: New Creation
author: Paul (attributed)

en: All this is from God, who reconciled us to himself through Christ and gave us the ministry of reconciliation—namely, that God was in Christ reconciling the world to himself, not counting their trespasses against them, and entrusting to us the word of reconciliation.
orig: τὰ δὲ πάντα ἐκ τοῦ θεοῦ τοῦ καταλλάξαντος ἡμᾶς ἑαυτῷ διὰ Χριστοῦ · καὶ δόντος ἡμῖν τὴν διακονίαν τῆς καταλλαγῆς · ὡς ὅτι θεὸς ἦν ἐν Χριστῷ κόσμον καταλλάσσων ἑαυτῷ · μὴ λογιζόμενος αὐτοῖς τὰ παραπτώματα αὐτῶν · καὶ θέμενος ἐν ἡμῖν τὸν λόγον τῆς καταλλαγῆς ·
title: 2 Corinthians 5:17-21: New Creation
author: Paul (attributed)

en: Therefore we are ambassadors on behalf of Christ, as though God were making his appeal through us. We implore you on behalf of Christ: be reconciled to God. The one who knew no sin, he made to be sin on our behalf, so that in him we might become the righteousness of God.
orig: ὑπὲρ Χριστοῦ οὖν πρεσβεύομεν · ὡς τοῦ θεοῦ παρακαλοῦντος διʼ ἡμῶν · δεόμεθα ὑπὲρ Χριστοῦ · καταλλάγητε τῷ θεῷ · τὸν μὴ γνόντα ἁμαρτίαν · ὑπὲρ ἡμῶν ἁμαρτίαν ἐποίησεν · ἵνα ἡμεῖς γενώμεθα δικαιοσύνη θεοῦ ἐν αὐτῷ ·
title: 2 Corinthians 5:17-21: New Creation
author: Paul (attributed)

en: For you know the grace of our Lord Jesus Christ, that though he was rich, for your sake he became poor, so that you by his poverty might become rich.
orig: γινώσκετε γὰρ τὴν χάριν τοῦ κυρίου ἡμῶν Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ · ὅτι διʼ ὑμᾶς ἐπτώχευσεν πλούσιος ὤν · ἵνα ὑμεῖς τῇ ἐκείνου πτωχείᾳ πλουτήσητε ·
title: 2 Corinthians 8:9: Rich Became Poor
author: Paul (attributed)

en: But God, being rich in mercy, because of the great love with which he loved us, even when we were dead in our trespasses, made us alive together with Christ—by grace you have been saved—and raised us up together and seated us together in the heavenly places in Christ Jesus, so that in the coming ages he might show the surpassing riches of his grace in kindness toward us in Christ Jesus.
orig: ὁ δὲ θεὸς πλούσιος ὢν ἐν ἐλέει · διὰ τὴν πολλὴν ἀγάπην αὐτοῦ ἣν ἠγάπησεν ἡμᾶς · καὶ ὄντας ἡμᾶς νεκροὺς τοῖς παραπτώμασιν · συνεζωοποίησεν τῷ Χριστῷ · χάριτί ἐστε σεσωσμένοι · καὶ συνήγειρεν καὶ συνεκάθισεν ἐν τοῖς ἐπουρανίοις ἐν Χριστῷ Ἰησοῦ · ἵνα ἐνδείξηται ἐν τοῖς αἰῶσιν τοῖς ἐπερχομένοις τὸ ὑπερβάλλον πλοῦτος τῆς χάριτος αὐτοῦ ἐν χρηστότητι ἐφʼ ἡμᾶς ἐν Χριστῷ Ἰησοῦ ·
title: Ephesians 2:4-10: Seated in the Heavenly Places
author: Paul (attributed)

en: For by grace you have been saved through faith, and this is not from yourselves—it is the gift of God—not from works, so that no one may boast. For we are his workmanship (poiēma, ποίημα), created in Christ Jesus for good works, which God prepared beforehand so that we would walk in them.
orig: τῇ γὰρ χάριτί ἐστε σεσωσμένοι διὰ πίστεως · καὶ τοῦτο οὐκ ἐξ ὑμῶν · θεοῦ τὸ δῶρον · οὐκ ἐξ ἔργων · ἵνα μή τις καυχήσηται · αὐτοῦ γάρ ἐσμεν ποίημα · κτισθέντες ἐν Χριστῷ Ἰησοῦ ἐπὶ ἔργοις ἀγαθοῖς · οἷς προητοίμασεν ὁ θεὸς ἵνα ἐν αὐτοῖς περιπατήσωμεν ·
title: Ephesians 2:4-10: Seated in the Heavenly Places
author: Paul (attributed)

en: And confessedly great is the mystery of godliness (eusebeia, εὐσέβεια): He who was manifested in the flesh, vindicated in the Spirit, seen by angels, proclaimed among the nations, believed in throughout the world, taken up in glory.
orig: καὶ ὁμολογουμένως μέγα ἐστὶν τὸ τῆς εὐσεβείας μυστήριον · ὃς ἐφανερώθη ἐν σαρκί · ἐδικαιώθη ἐν πνεύματι · ὤφθη ἀγγέλοις · ἐκηρύχθη ἐν ἔθνεσιν · ἐπιστεύθη ἐν κόσμῳ · ἀνελήμφθη ἐν δόξῃ ·
title: 1 Timothy 3:16: The Mystery of Godliness
author: Paul (attributed)

en: But when the kindness and love for humanity (philanthrōpia, φιλανθρωπία) of God our Savior appeared, he saved us, not from works done in righteousness that we had performed, but according to his mercy, through the washing of regeneration (palingenesia, παλιγγενεσία) and renewal of the Holy Spirit, which he poured out upon us richly through Jesus Christ our Savior, so that, having been justified by his grace, we might become heirs according to the hope of eternal life.
orig: ὅτε δὲ ἡ χρηστότης καὶ ἡ φιλανθρωπία ἐπεφάνη τοῦ σωτῆρος ἡμῶν θεοῦ · οὐκ ἐξ ἔργων τῶν ἐν δικαιοσύνῃ ἃ ἐποιήσαμεν ἡμεῖς · ἀλλὰ κατὰ τὸ αὐτοῦ ἔλεος ἔσωσεν ἡμᾶς · διὰ λουτροῦ παλιγγενεσίας καὶ ἀνακαινώσεως πνεύματος ἁγίου · οὗ ἐξέχεεν ἐφʼ ἡμᾶς πλουσίως διὰ Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ τοῦ σωτῆρος ἡμῶν · ἵνα δικαιωθέντες τῇ ἐκείνου χάριτι · κληρονόμοι γενηθῶμεν κατʼ ἐλπίδα ζωῆς αἰωνίου ·
title: Titus 3:4-7: Washing of Regeneration
author: Paul (attributed)

en: Every good gift and every perfect gift is from above, coming down from the Father of lights, with whom there is no variation or shadow of turning.
orig: πᾶσα δόσις ἀγαθὴ καὶ πᾶν δώρημα τέλειον ἄνωθέν ἐστιν · καταβαῖνον ἀπὸ τοῦ πατρὸς τῶν φώτων · παρʼ ὧ οὐκ ἔνι παραλλαγὴ ἢ τροπῆς ἀποσκίασμα ·
title: James 1:17-18: Brought Forth by the Word of Truth
author: John (attributed)

en: Of his own will he brought us forth (apekuēsen, ἀπεκύησεν) by the word of truth, that we should be a kind of firstfruits (aparchē, ἀπαρχή) of his creatures.
orig: βουληθεὶς ἀπεκύησεν ἡμᾶς λόγῳ ἀληθείας · εἰς τὸ εἶναι ἡμᾶς ἀπαρχήν τινα τῶν αὐτοῦ κτισμάτων ·
title: James 1:17-18: Brought Forth by the Word of Truth
author: John (attributed)

en: For God created man for incorruption (aphtharsia, ἀφθαρσία), and made him in the image of his own eternity (aidiotētos, ἀϊδιότητος).
orig: ὅτι ὁ θεὸς ἔκτισεν τὸν ἄνθρωπον ἐπʼ ἀφθαρσίᾳ · καὶ εἰκόνα τῆς ἰδίας ἀϊδιότητος ἐποίησεν αὐτόν ·
title: Wisdom 2:23-24: Created for Incorruption
author: Solomon (attributed)

en: But through the envy of the devil, death entered the world, and those who belong to his portion experience it.
orig: φθόνῳ δὲ διαβόλου θάνατος εἰσῆλθεν εἰς τὸν κόσμον · πειράζουσιν δὲ αὐτὸν οἱ τῆς ἐκείνου μερίδος ὄντες ·
title: Wisdom 2:23-24: Created for Incorruption
author: Solomon (attributed)

en: The Lord created man out of earth, and turned him back to it again. He gave them a fixed number of days, and granted them authority over everything on earth.
orig: κύριος ἔκτισεν ἐκ γῆς ἄνθρωπον · καὶ πάλιν ἐπέστρεψεν αὐτὸν εἰς αὐτήν · ἡμέρας ἀριθμοῦ καὶ καιρὸν ἔδωκεν αὐτοῖς · καὶ ἔδωκεν αὐτοῖς ἐξουσίαν τῶν ἐπʼ αὐτῆς ·
title: Sirach 17:1-4: Strength Like His Own
author: Solomon (attributed)

en: He endowed them with strength like his own, and made them in his own image. He put the fear of them upon all flesh, and gave them dominion over beasts and birds.
orig: καθʼ ἑαυτὸν ἐνέδυσεν αὐτούς ἰσχύν · καὶ κατʼ εἰκόνα αὐτοῦ ἐποίησεν αὐτούς · ἔθηκεν τὸν φόβον αὐτοῦ ἐπὶ πάσης σαρκός · καὶ κατακυριεύειν θηρίων καὶ πετεινῶν ·
title: Sirach 17:1-4: Strength Like His Own
author: Solomon (attributed)

en: Then I saw a new heaven and a new earth, for the first heaven and the first earth had passed away, and the sea was no more. And I saw the holy city, new Jerusalem, coming down out of heaven from God, prepared as a bride adorned for her husband.
orig: καὶ εἶδον οὐρανὸν καινὸν καὶ γῆν καινήν · ὁ γὰρ πρῶτος οὐρανὸς καὶ ἡ πρώτη γῆ ἀπῆλθαν · καὶ ἡ θάλασσα οὐκ ἔστιν ἔτι · καὶ τὴν πόλιν τὴν ἁγίαν Ἰερουσαλὴμ καινὴν εἶδον καταβαίνουσαν ἐκ τοῦ οὐρανοῦ ἀπὸ τοῦ θεοῦ · ἡτοιμασμένην ὡς νύμφην κεκοσμημένην τῷ ἀνδρὶ αὐτῆς ·
title: Revelation 21:1-7: I Make All Things New
author: John (attributed)

en: And I heard a loud voice from the throne saying, “Behold, the dwelling of God is with humanity, and he will dwell with them, and they shall be his peoples, and God himself will be with them as their God. He will wipe away every tear from their eyes, and death shall be no more, neither shall there be mourning, nor crying, nor pain anymore, for the former things have passed away.”
orig: καὶ ἤκουσα φωνῆς μεγάλης ἐκ τοῦ θρόνου λεγούσης · ἰδοὺ ἡ σκηνὴ τοῦ θεοῦ μετὰ τῶν ἀνθρώπων · καὶ σκηνώσει μετʼ αὐτῶν · καὶ αὐτοὶ λαοὶ αὐτοῦ ἔσονται · καὶ αὐτὸς ὁ θεὸς μετʼ αὐτῶν ἔσται αὐτῶν θεός · καὶ ἐξαλείψει πᾶν δάκρυον ἐκ τῶν ὀφθαλμῶν αὐτῶν · καὶ ὁ θάνατος οὐκ ἔσται ἔτι · οὔτε πένθος οὔτε κραυγὴ οὔτε πόνος οὐκ ἔσται ἔτι · τὰ πρῶτα ἀπῆλθαν ·
title: Revelation 21:1-7: I Make All Things New
author: John (attributed)

en: And the one seated on the throne said, “Behold, I make all things new.” And he said, “Write, for these words are faithful and true.” And he said to me, “It is done. I am the Alpha and the Omega, the beginning and the end. To the one who thirsts I will give from the spring of the water of life without payment. The one who conquers shall inherit these things, and I will be his God and he shall be my son.”
orig: καὶ εἶπεν ὁ καθήμενος ἐπὶ τῷ θρόνῳ · ἰδοὺ καινὰ ποιῶ πάντα · καὶ λέγει · γράψον ὅτι οὗτοι οἱ λόγοι πιστοὶ καὶ ἀληθινοί εἰσιν · καὶ εἶπέν μοι · γέγοναν · ἐγώ εἰμι τὸ ἄλφα καὶ τὸ ὦ · ἡ ἀρχὴ καὶ τὸ τέλος · ἐγὼ τῷ διψῶντι δώσω ἐκ τῆς πηγῆς τοῦ ὕδατος τῆς ζωῆς δωρεάν · ὁ νικῶν κληρονομήσει ταῦτα · καὶ ἔσομαι αὐτῷ θεὸς καὶ αὐτὸς ἔσται μοι υἱός ·
title: Revelation 21:1-7: I Make All Things New
author: John (attributed)

en: Then he showed me the river of the water of life, bright as crystal, flowing from the throne of God and of the Lamb through the middle of the street of the city. On either side of the river was the tree of life, bearing twelve kinds of fruit, yielding its fruit each month; and the leaves of the tree were for the healing of the nations.
orig: καὶ ἔδειξέν μοι ποταμὸν ὕδατος ζωῆς λαμπρὸν ὡς κρύσταλλον · ἐκπορευόμενον ἐκ τοῦ θρόνου τοῦ θεοῦ καὶ τοῦ ἀρνίου · ἐν μέσῳ τῆς πλατείας αὐτῆς · καὶ τοῦ ποταμοῦ ἐντεῦθεν καὶ ἐκεῖθεν ξύλον ζωῆς · ποιοῦν καρποὺς δώδεκα · κατὰ μῆνα ἕκαστον ἀποδιδοῦν τὸν καρπὸν αὐτοῦ · καὶ τὰ φύλλα τοῦ ξύλου εἰς θεραπείαν τῶν ἐθνῶν ·
title: Revelation 22:1-5: They Shall See His Face
author: John (attributed)

en: No longer will there be anything accursed. The throne of God and of the Lamb will be in it, and his servants will worship him. They shall see his face, and his name shall be on their foreheads. And night will be no more; they will need no light of lamp or sun, for the Lord God will be their light, and they will reign forever and ever.
orig: καὶ πᾶν κατάθεμα οὐκ ἔσται ἔτι · καὶ ὁ θρόνος τοῦ θεοῦ καὶ τοῦ ἀρνίου ἐν αὐτῇ ἔσται · καὶ οἱ δοῦλοι αὐτοῦ λατρεύσουσιν αὐτῷ · καὶ ὄψονται τὸ πρόσωπον αὐτοῦ · καὶ τὸ ὄνομα αὐτοῦ ἐπὶ τῶν μετώπων αὐτῶν · καὶ νὺξ οὐκ ἔσται ἔτι · καὶ οὐκ ἔχουσιν χρείαν φωτὸς λύχνου καὶ φωτὸς ἡλίου · ὅτι κύριος ὁ θεὸς φωτιεῖ αὐτούς · καὶ βασιλεύσουσιν εἰς τοὺς αἰῶνας τῶν αἰώνων ·
title: Revelation 22:1-5: They Shall See His Face
author: John (attributed)

en: For in him we live and move and have our being, as also some of your own poets have said, “For we are indeed his offspring (genos, γένος).”
orig: ἐν αὐτῷ γὰρ ζῶμεν καὶ κινούμεθα καὶ ἐσμέν · ὡς καί τινες τῶν καθʼ ὑμᾶς ποιητῶν εἰρήκασιν · τοῦ γὰρ καὶ γένος ἐσμέν ·
title: Acts 17:28-29: In Him We Live and Move
author: Paul (attributed)

en: Being then God’s offspring, we ought not to think that the divine nature (to theion, τὸ θεῖον) is like gold or silver or stone, an image formed by the art and imagination of man.
orig: γένος οὖν ὑπάρχοντες τοῦ θεοῦ · οὐκ ὀφείλομεν νομίζειν χρυσῷ ἢ ἀργύρῳ ἢ λίθῳ · χαράγματι τέχνης καὶ ἐνθυμήσεως ἀνθρώπου · τὸ θεῖον εἶναι ὅμοιον ·
title: Acts 17:28-29: In Him We Live and Move
author: Paul (attributed)

en: Jesus saw infants being nursed. He said to his disciples, “These nursing infants are like those who enter the kingdom.” They said to him, “Shall we then enter the kingdom as infants?”
orig: ⲉ̀ⲥ ̄ⲉⲣ̄ⲉ ⲁ̄ⲥⲕⲉⲉ ⲓ̄̄ⲁⲥⲓⲁⲕⲉ ⲓⲉⲥⲓⲁ̄
title: Gospel of Thomas, Saying 22: When You Make the Two One
author: Unknown (Gospel of Thomas)

en: Jesus said to them, “When you make the two one, and when you make the inside like the outside and the outside like the inside, and the above like the below, and when you make the male and the female into a single one, so that the male will not be male nor the female be female—”
orig: ⲓ̄̄ⲉ ⲓ̄̄ⲁⲕⲥ ⲓ̄̄ ⲁⲥⲣ ⲁⲥⲉ ⲓⲉⲟⲉⲁ ⲓ̄̄ⲁⲥ
title: Gospel of Thomas, Saying 22: When You Make the Two One
author: Unknown (Gospel of Thomas)

en: “—when you make eyes in place of an eye, and a hand in place of a hand, and a foot in place of a foot, an image (eikon) in place of an image—then you will enter the kingdom.”
orig: ⲓ̄̄ⲁⲥⲕⲉⲉ ⲓ̄̄ⲉⲓⲉⲓⲉ ⲓ̄̄ⲁⲥ
title: Gospel of Thomas, Saying 22: When You Make the Two One
author: Unknown (Gospel of Thomas)

en: Jesus said, “Whoever drinks from my mouth will become like me. I myself shall become that person, and the hidden things will be revealed to that one.”
orig: ⲓ̄̄ⲉ ⲓ̄̄ⲁ ⲓ̄̄ⲉⲥⲕⲉⲉ ⲓ̄̄ⲉⲓⲉⲓⲉ
title: Gospel of Thomas, Saying 108: Become Like Me
author: Unknown (Gospel of Thomas)

en: I am the Word who dwells in the ineffable Silence. I dwell in undefiled Light, and a Thought revealed itself perceptibly through the great Speech of the Mother, although a male offspring supports me as my foundation.
orig: ⲁⲥⲟⲕ ⲓ̄̄ⲉⲥⲉⲁⲥⲉ ⲓ̄̄ⲉⲥⲉ ⲁⲥⲉⲓ̄̄ⲉ
title: Trimorphic Protennoia: The First Thought Descends
author: Unknown (Gospel of Thomas)

en: I put on Jesus. I bore him from the cursed tree and established him in the dwelling places of his Father. And those who guard their dwelling places did not recognise me, for I am unrestrainable together with my seed.
orig: ⲁⲥⲉⲓ̄̄ⲉ ⲓ̄̄ⲁⲥⲉⲥⲉ ⲓ̄̄ⲉⲥⲓ̄̄ⲉ
title: Trimorphic Protennoia: The First Thought Descends
author: Unknown (Gospel of Thomas)

en: I am the life of my offspring (Epinoia). I am the invisible one within the All. I am the one who gradually gives form to the All. I am the Mother of the Voice, speaking in many ways, completing the All.
orig: ⲁⲥⲟⲕ ⲓ̄̄ⲉ ⲓ̄̄ⲁⲥⲉ ⲓ̄̄ⲉⲥⲉⲥ
title: Trimorphic Protennoia: The First Thought Descends
author: Unknown (Gospel of Thomas)

en: I was sent forth from the Power, and I have come to those who reflect upon me, and I have been found among those who seek after me. Look upon me, you who reflect upon me, and you hearers, hear me.
orig: ⲁⲥⲉⲥⲓ̄̄ⲉ ⲓ̄̄ⲁⲥⲉ ⲓ̄̄ⲉⲥⲉⲁⲥⲉ
title: Thunder, Perfect Mind: I Am the First and the Last
author: Unknown (Gospel of Thomas)

en: I am the first and the last. I am the honored one and the scorned one. I am the whore and the holy one. I am the wife and the virgin. I am the mother and the daughter.
orig: ⲁⲥⲟⲕ ⲓ̄̄ⲉⲥⲉ ⲁⲥⲉ ⲓ̄̄ⲉⲥⲉ ⲓ̄̄ⲁⲥⲉ
title: Thunder, Perfect Mind: I Am the First and the Last
author: Unknown (Gospel of Thomas)

en: I am the silence that is incomprehensible and the idea whose remembrance is frequent. I am the voice whose sound is manifold and the word whose appearance is multiple. I am the utterance of my name.
orig: ⲁⲥⲟⲕ ⲓ̄̄ⲉⲥⲉⲁⲥ ⲓ̄̄ⲉⲥⲉ ⲓ̄̄ⲉⲥ
title: Thunder, Perfect Mind: I Am the First and the Last
author: Unknown (Gospel of Thomas)

en: I am knowledge and ignorance. I am shame and boldness. I am shameless; I am ashamed. I am strength and I am fear. I am war and peace. Give heed to me.
orig: ⲁⲥⲟⲕ ⲓ̄̄ⲉⲥⲉ ⲓ̄̄ⲉⲥⲉⲁⲥ ⲓ̄̄ⲉⲥ
title: Thunder, Perfect Mind: I Am the First and the Last
author: Unknown (Gospel of Thomas)

en: In those days, in those years, the sage of Eridu, Ea created him as a model of men. The sage—his command no one could violate—the capable, the most wise among the Anunnaki was he. Blameless, with clean hands, the anointed one, the observer of rites.
orig: i-nu-mi-šu i-na ša-na-a-ti šu-a-ti-ma ap-kal E-ri-du-ga dÉ-a ib-na-aš-šu ki-ma a-wi-lu-ti-šu u-ša-al-li-mu
title: Myth of Adapa: Immortality Refused
author: Anonymous (Babylonian)

en: Ea spoke to him: “Adapa, you are going before Anu the king. When you go up to heaven, when you approach the gate of Anu, the food of death they will set before you—do not eat. The water of death they will set before you—do not drink.”
orig: dÉ-a i-pu-ul-šu a-da-pa at-ta a-na pa-ni dĀ-nim šar-ri tal-la-ku a-ka-la mu-ti i-na-an-din-nak-ku-nu-ši la ta-a-kal
title: Myth of Adapa: Immortality Refused
author: Anonymous (Babylonian)

en: Anu looked at him and laughed at him: “Come, Adapa, why did you not eat, why did you not drink? Now you shall not have eternal life!” Adapa answered: “Ea my lord commanded me: ‘Do not eat, do not drink.’” Anu said: “Take him and return him to his earth.”
orig: dĀ-nu i-ṭa-am-mi-šu i-ṣi-ḥ-šu ta-a a-da-pa a-mi-ni la ta-a-kal a-mi-ni la ta-aš-ti ba-la-ṭa la ti-i-di
title: Myth of Adapa: Immortality Refused
author: Anonymous (Babylonian)

en: To the Land of No Return, the realm of Ereshkigal, Ishtar daughter of Sin set her mind. The daughter of Sin set her mind to the dark house, the dwelling of Irkalla, to the house which none who enter leave, to the road from which there is no way back.
orig: a-na KUR.NU.GI₄ qa-aq-qa-ri la ta-a-ri dŠTAR mārat d30 u-zu-un-ša iš-ku-un
title: Descent of Ishtar: Stripped of Divinity
author: Anonymous (Babylonian)

en: When she arrived at the gate of the Land of No Return, she said to the gatekeeper: “Open your gate for me, open your gate that I may enter! If you do not open the gate so that I may enter, I will smash the door, I will shatter the bolt, I will raise up the dead and they shall eat the living!”
orig: a-na bāb KUR.NU.GI₄ i-na ka-ša-di-ša a-na a-ti-i bā-bi a-mat iz-za-kar pi-ta-a bā-ba-ka lu-ru-ba a-na-ku
title: Descent of Ishtar: Stripped of Divinity
author: Anonymous (Babylonian)

en: At the first gate he removed the great crown from her head. “Why, gatekeeper, have you taken the great crown from my head?” “Enter, my lady. The rules of the Mistress of the Underworld are thus.” At the second gate he removed her earrings. At the third gate he removed her necklace. At the fourth gate he removed the ornaments from her breast. At the fifth gate he removed the girdle of birthstones from her waist. At the sixth gate he removed the bangles from her wrists and ankles. At the seventh gate he removed the garment of her body.
orig: i-na bābi iš-te-en šu-šu-ra-a agû-a rabî-ta ša qa-qa-di-ša u-še-li am-mi-ni at-mu-u bā-bu agû-a rabî-ta ša qa-qa-di-ia tu-še-li
title: Descent of Ishtar: Stripped of Divinity
author: Anonymous (Babylonian)

en: When Ishtar had descended to the Land of No Return, Ereshkigal saw her and was enraged at her presence. Ishtar, without thinking, rushed at her. Ereshkigal opened her mouth and spoke, to Namtar her vizier she gave the command: “Strike her! Loose against her the sixty miseries!”
orig: dŠTAR i-na e-re-bi-ša a-na KUR.NU.GI₄ dEreš-ki-gal i-mu-rši-ma i-na pa-ni-ša i-zi-iz
title: Descent of Ishtar: Stripped of Divinity
author: Anonymous (Babylonian)

en: O King, you have not departed dead; you have departed alive. Sit upon the throne of Osiris, with your sceptre in your hand, that you may give commands to the living.
orig: j wnis n km.nḵ m mwt km.nḵ m ˆnḫ ḥms ḥr ns.t Wsjr ˆbā.k m-ˆ.k wd mdw n ˆnḫw
title: Pyramid Text Utterance 217: The King Becomes a Star
author: Anonymous (Egyptian)

en: Your arm is that of Atum, your shoulders are those of Atum, your belly is that of Atum, your back is that of Atum, your legs are those of Atum, your face is that of Anubis.
orig: ˆ.k pw n Itmw qāḥ.wy.k pw n Itmw ḫ.t.k pw nt Itmw pḥ.k pw n Itmw rdwy.k pw n Itmw ḥr.k pw n Jnpw
title: Pyramid Text Utterance 217: The King Becomes a Star
author: Anonymous (Egyptian)

en: The regions of Horus serve you. The regions of Seth serve you. You go about the regions, you traverse the Two Lands, as a star which goes across the sea. The Netherworld has grasped your hand at the place where Orion is.
orig: spā.wt Ḥr šms.sn tw spā.wt Stš šms.sn tw phr.k spā.wt dā.k tā.wy m sbā dā wāḏ-wr
title: Pyramid Text Utterance 217: The King Becomes a Star
author: Anonymous (Egyptian)

en: When kingship descended from heaven, kingship was in Eridu. In Eridu, Alulim became king; he ruled for 28,800 years. Alalgar ruled for 36,000 years. Two kings ruled for 64,800 years. Then Eridu fell, and the kingship was taken to Bad-tibira.
orig: nam-lugal an-ta e₂-de₃-a-ba nam-lugal-la eriduᵏᴵ-ga a-lu-lim lugal mu 28800 i₃-ak
title: Sumerian King List: When Kingship Descended from Heaven
author: Anonymous (Sumerian)

en: In Bad-tibira, En-men-lu-ana ruled for 43,200 years. En-men-gal-ana ruled for 28,800 years. The divine Dumuzi, the shepherd, ruled for 36,000 years. Three kings ruled for 108,000 years. Then Bad-tibira fell, and the kingship was taken to Larak.
orig: bad₃-tibiraᵏᴵ en-men-lu-an-na mu 43200 i₃-ak en-men-gal-an-na mu 28800 i₃-ak dDumu-zi sipa mu 36000 i₃-ak
title: Sumerian King List: When Kingship Descended from Heaven
author: Anonymous (Sumerian)

en: Then the Flood swept over. After the Flood had swept over, and the kingship had descended from heaven again, the kingship was in Kish.
orig: a-ma-ru ba-ur₃-ra-ta a-ma-ru ba-ur₃-ra-ta nam-lugal an-ta e₂-de₃-a-ba nam-lugal-la kišᵏᴵ-a
title: Sumerian King List: When Kingship Descended from Heaven
author: Anonymous (Sumerian)

en: Now there was a man of the Pharisees named Nicodemus, a ruler of the Jews. He came to him at night and said to him, “Rabbi, we know that you have come from God as a teacher, for no one can do these signs that you do unless God is with him.”
orig: ἦν δὲ ἄνθρωπος ἐκ τῶν Φαρισαίων Νικόδημος ὄνομα αὐτῷ · ἄρχων τῶν Ιουδαίων · οὗτος ἦλθεν προς αὐτὸν νυκτὸς καὶ εἶπεν αὐτῷ · ῥαββεί · οἴδαμεν ὅτι ἀπὸ θεοῦ ἐλήλυθας διδάσκαλος ·
title: John 3:1-8: Born Again from Above
author: John (attributed)

en: Jesus answered and said to him, “Truly, truly, I say to you: unless one is born from above (anothen, ἄνωθεν), one cannot see the kingdom of God.” Nicodemus said to him, “How can a man be born when he is old? Can he enter a second time into his mother’s womb and be born?”
orig: ἀπεκρίθη Ιησοῦς καὶ εἶπεν αὐτῷ · ἀμὴν ἀμὴν λέγω σοι · ἐὰν μή τις γεννηθῇ ἄνωθεν · οὐ δύναται ἰδεῖν τὴν βασιλείαν τοῦ θεοῦ ·
title: John 3:1-8: Born Again from Above
author: John (attributed)

en: Jesus answered, “Truly, truly, I say to you: unless one is born of water and Spirit, one cannot enter the kingdom of God. What is born of the flesh is flesh, and what is born of the Spirit is spirit.”
orig: ἀπεκρίθη Ιησοῦς · ἀμὴν ἀμὴν λέγω σοι · ἐὰν μή τις γεννηθῇ ἐξ ὕδατος καὶ πνεύματος · οὐ δύναται εἰσελθεῖν εἰς τὴν βασιλείαν τοῦ θεοῦ ·
title: John 3:1-8: Born Again from Above
author: John (attributed)

en: “Do not marvel that I said to you: you must be born from above. The wind (pneuma, πνεῦμα) blows where it wills, and you hear its sound, but you do not know where it comes from or where it goes. So it is with everyone who is born of the Spirit.”
orig: μὴ θαυμάσῃς ὅτι εἶπον σοι · δεῖ ὑμᾶς γεννηθῆναι ἄνωθεν · τὸ πνεῦμα ὅπου θέλει πνεῖ · καὶ τὴν φωνὴν αὐτοῦ ἀκούεις · ἀλλ’ οὐκ οἶδας πόθεν ἔρχεται καὶ ποῦ ὑπάγει ·
title: John 3:1-8: Born Again from Above
author: John (attributed)

en: So Jesus said to them, “Truly, truly, I say to you: unless you eat the flesh of the Son of Man and drink his blood, you have no life in you. Whoever feeds on (trogon, τρώγων) my flesh and drinks my blood has eternal life, and I will raise him up on the last day.”
orig: εἶπεν οὖν αὐτοῖς ὁ Ιησοῦς · ἀμὴν ἀμὴν λέγω ὑμῖν · ἐὰν μὴ φάγητε τὴν σάρκα τοῦ υἱοῦ τοῦ ἀνθρώπου καὶ πίητε αὐτοῦ τὸ αἷμα · οὐκ ἔχετε ζωὴν ἐν ἑαυτοῖς · ὁ τρώγων μου τὴν σάρκα καὶ πίνων μου τὸ αἷμα ἔχει ζωὴν αἰώνιον ·
title: John 6:53-58: Eat My Flesh, Drink My Blood
author: John (attributed)

en: “For my flesh is true food, and my blood is true drink. Whoever feeds on my flesh and drinks my blood abides in me, and I in him.”
orig: ἡ γὰρ σάρξ μου ἀληθής ἐστιν βρῶσις · καὶ τὸ αἷμά μου ἀληθής ἐστιν πόσις · ὁ τρώγων μου τὴν σάρκα καὶ πίνων μου τὸ αἷμα ἐν ἐμοὶ μένει κἀγὼ ἐν αὐτῷ ·
title: John 6:53-58: Eat My Flesh, Drink My Blood
author: John (attributed)

en: “As the living Father sent me, and I live because of the Father, so whoever feeds on me will also live because of me. This is the bread that came down from heaven, not like the bread the fathers ate, and died. Whoever feeds on this bread will live forever.”
orig: καθὼς ἀπέστειλέν με ὁ ζῶν πατὴρ κἀγὼ ζῶ διὰ τὸν πατέρα · καὶ ὁ τρώγων με κἀκεῖνος ζήσει δι’ ἐμέ · οὗτός ἐστιν ὁ ἄρτος ὁ ἐξ οὐρανοῦ καταβάς ·
title: John 6:53-58: Eat My Flesh, Drink My Blood
author: John (attributed)

en: And after six days Jesus took with him Peter and James and John his brother, and led them up a high mountain by themselves. And he was transfigured (metemorphothe, μετεμορφώθη) before them, and his face shone like the sun, and his garments became white as light.
orig: καὶ μεθ’ ἡμέρας ἓξ παραλαμβάνει ὁ Ιησοῦς τὸν Πέτρον καὶ Ιάκωβον καὶ Ιωάννην τὸν ἀδελφὸν αὐτοῦ · καὶ ἀναφέρει αὐτοὺς εἰς ὄρος ὑψηλὸν κατ’ ἰδίαν · καὶ μετεμορφώθη ἔμπροσθεν αὐτῶν · καὶ ἔλαμψεν τὸ πρόσωπον αὐτοῦ ὡς ὁ ἥλιος ·
title: Matthew 17:1-8: The Transfiguration
author: Matthew (attributed)

en: And behold, there appeared to them Moses and Elijah, talking with him. And Peter said to Jesus, “Lord, it is good that we are here. If you wish, I will make three tents here: one for you, one for Moses, and one for Elijah.”
orig: καὶ ἰδοὺ ὤφθη αὐτοῖς Μωυσῆς καὶ Ηλείας συνλαλοῦντες μετ’ αὐτοῦ · ἀποκριθεὶς δὲ ὁ Πέτρος εἶπεν τῷ Ιησοῦ · κύριε καλόν ἐστιν ἡμᾶς ὥδε εἶναι ·
title: Matthew 17:1-8: The Transfiguration
author: Matthew (attributed)

en: While he was still speaking, behold, a bright cloud overshadowed them, and a voice from the cloud said, “This is my beloved Son, with whom I am well pleased. Listen to him.” When the disciples heard this, they fell on their faces and were greatly afraid.
orig: ἔτι αὐτοῦ λαλοῦντος ἰδοὺ νεφέλη φωτεινὴ ἐπεσκίασεν αὐτούς · καὶ ἰδοὺ φωνὴ ἐκ τῆς νεφέλης λέγουσα · οὗτός ἐστιν ὁ υἱός μου ὁ ἀγαπητός · ἐν ἷ εὐδόκησα · ἀκούετε αὐτοῦ ·
title: Matthew 17:1-8: The Transfiguration
author: Matthew (attributed)

en: But Jesus came and touched them, saying, “Rise, and do not be afraid.” And when they lifted up their eyes, they saw no one but Jesus only.
orig: καὶ προσῆλθεν ὁ Ιησοῦς καὶ ἁψάμενος αὐτῶν εἶπεν · ἐγέρθητε καὶ μὴ φοβεῖσθε · ἐπάραντες δὲ τοὺς ὀφθαλμοὺς αὐτῶν οὐδένα εἶδον εἰ μὴ αὐτὸν Ιησοῦν μόνον ·
title: Matthew 17:1-8: The Transfiguration
author: Matthew (attributed)

en: And behold, on that same day two of them were going to a village named Emmaus, about sixty stadia from Jerusalem. And they were talking with each other about all these things that had happened. While they were talking and discussing together, Jesus himself drew near and went with them. But their eyes were kept from recognising him.
orig: καὶ ἰδοὺ δύο ἐξ αὐτῶν ἐν αὐτῇ τῇ ἡμέρᾳ ἦσαν πορευόμενοι εἰς κώμην ἀπέχουσαν σταδίους ἑξήκοντα ἀπὸ Ιερουσαλήμ ·
title: Luke 24:13-35: The Road to Emmaus
author: Luke (attributed)

en: And he said to them, “O foolish ones, and slow of heart to believe all that the prophets have spoken! Was it not necessary that the Christ should suffer these things and enter into his glory?” And beginning with Moses and all the Prophets, he interpreted to them in all the Scriptures the things concerning himself.
orig: καὶ αὐτὸς εἶπεν πρὸς αὐτούς · ὦ ἀνόητοι καὶ βραδεῖς τῇ καρδίᾳ τοῦ πιστεύειν ἐπὶ πᾶσιν οἷς ἐλάλησαν οἱ προφῆται ·
title: Luke 24:13-35: The Road to Emmaus
author: Luke (attributed)

en: So they drew near to the village to which they were going. He acted as though he were going farther, but they urged him strongly, saying, “Stay with us, for it is toward evening and the day is now far spent.” So he went in to stay with them. When he was at table with them, he took the bread and blessed it and broke it and gave it to them. And their eyes were opened, and they recognised him. And he vanished from their sight.
orig: καὶ ἤγγισαν εἰς τὴν κώμην οὗ ἐπορεύοντο · καὶ αὐτὸς προσεποιήσατο πορρώτερον πορεύεσθαι ·
title: Luke 24:13-35: The Road to Emmaus
author: Luke (attributed)

en: They said to each other, “Did not our hearts burn within us while he talked to us on the road, while he opened to us the Scriptures?” And they rose that same hour and returned to Jerusalem. And they found the eleven and those who were with them gathered together, saying, “The Lord has risen indeed, and has appeared to Simon!” Then they told what had happened on the road, and how he was known to them in the breaking of the bread.
orig: καὶ εἶπαν πρὸς ἀλλήλους · οὐχὶ ἡ καρδία ἡμῶν καιομένη ἦν ἐν ἡμῖν · ὡς ἐλάλει ἡμῖν ἐν τῇ ὁδῷ · ὡς διήνοιγεν ἡμῖν τὰς γραφάς ·
title: Luke 24:13-35: The Road to Emmaus
author: Luke (attributed)

en: I appeal to you therefore, brothers, by the mercies of God, to present your bodies (somata, σώματα) as a living sacrifice, holy and acceptable to God, which is your reasonable worship (logiken latreian, λογικὴν λατρείαν).
orig: παρακαλῶ οὖν ὑμᾶς ἀδελφοί · διὰ τῶν οἰκτιρμῶν τοῦ θεοῦ · παραστῆσαι τὰ σώματα ὑμῶν θυσίαν ζῶσαν ἁγίαν εὐάρεστον τῷ θεῷ · τὴν λογικὴν λατρείαν ὑμῶν ·
title: Romans 12:1-2: Transformed by Renewing of Mind
author: Paul (attributed)

en: Do not be conformed to this age (aion, αἰών), but be transformed (metamorphousthe, μεταμορφοῦσθε) by the renewing of your mind (nous, νοῦς), so that you may discern what is the will of God—what is good and acceptable and perfect.
