orig: καὶ μὴ συσχηματίζεσθε τῷ αἰῶνι τούτῳ · ἀλλὰ μεταμορφοῦσθε τῇ ἀνακαινώσει τοῦ νοός · εἰς τὸ δοκιμάζειν ὑμᾶς τί τὸ θέλημα τοῦ θεοῦ · τὸ ἀγαθὸν καὶ εὐάρεστον καὶ τέλειον ·
title: Romans 12:1-2: Transformed by Renewing of Mind
author: Paul (attributed)

en: For God, who said, “Let light shine out of darkness,” has shone in our hearts to give the light of the knowledge of the glory of God in the face of Jesus Christ.
orig: ὅτι ὁ θεὸς ὁ εἰπών · ἐκ σκότους φῶς λάμψει · ὃς ἔλαμψεν ἐν ταῖς καρδίαις ἡμῶν · πρὸς φωτισμὸν τῆς γνώσεως τῆς δόξης τοῦ θεοῦ ἐν προσώπῳ Ιησοῦ Χριστοῦ ·
title: 2 Corinthians 4:6-7: Light in Our Hearts
author: Paul (attributed)

en: But we have this treasure in earthen vessels (ostrakinois skeuesin, ὀστρακίνοις σκεύεσιν), so that the surpassing greatness of the power may be of God and not from us.
orig: ἔχομεν δὲ τὸν θησαυρὸν τοῦτον ἐν ὀστρακίνοις σκεύεσιν · ἵνα ἡ ὑπερβολὴ τῆς δυνάμεως ᾖ τοῦ θεοῦ καὶ μὴ ἐξ ἡμῶν ·
title: 2 Corinthians 4:6-7: Light in Our Hearts
author: Paul (attributed)

en: The divine nature did not change when it assumed a human body and a human soul. Remaining in its own essence unchanged, it descended to human affairs out of its love for humankind. We do not say that God suffered change or transformation, for the divine Word remained divine Word in essence.
orig: οὐ μεταβολὴν ὑπέμεινεν ἡ θεία φύσις αὐτοῦ σῶμα ἀνθρώπινον καὶ ψυχὴν ἀνθρωπίνην ἀναλαβοῦσα · μένουσα δὲ ἐν τῇ ἑαυτῆς οὐσίᾳ κατελήλυθεν εἰς τὰ ἀνθρώπινα
title: Contra Celsum III.28: Why God Became Man
author: Origen of Alexandria

en: For if, as even the Greeks admit, the providence of God pervades all things without being defiled by any, why should it be thought incredible that the divine nature touched a human body and human soul without thereby being contaminated? Just as the sun’s rays touch filth without being polluted, so the divine nature assumed humanity without undergoing any defilement.
orig: εἰ γὰρ καὶ κατὰ τοὺς Ἕλληνας ἡ πρόνοια τοῦ θεοῦ διήκει διὰ πάντων οὐ μολυνομένη ὑπὸ οὐδενός
title: Contra Celsum III.28: Why God Became Man
author: Origen of Alexandria

en: For he was made man that we might be made God (theopoiethomen, θεοποιηθῶμεν); and he manifested himself through a body that we might receive the idea of the unseen Father; and he endured the insolence of men that we might inherit immortality.
orig: αὐτὸς γὰρ ἐνηνθρώπησεν ἵνα ἡμεῖς θεοποιηθῶμεν · καὶ ἐφανέρωσεν ἑαυτὸν διὰ σώματος ἵνα ἡμεῖς τοῦ ἀοράτου πατρὸς ἔννοιαν λάβωμεν ·
title: Contra Arianos I.39: Made Man That We Might Be Made God
author: Athanasius of Alexandria

en: For he himself was not diminished by bearing our body, but rather he deified (etheopoiesen, ἐθεοποίησεν) that which he put on, and further gave it graciously to the race of humankind.
orig: αὐτὸς γὰρ οὐκ ἐλαττώθη φορῶν τὸ σῶμα ἡμῶν · ἀλλὰ μᾶλλον ἐθεοποίησεν ὃ ἐνεδύσατο · καὶ προσέτι ἐχαρίσατο τοῦτο τῷ γένει τῶν ἀνθρώπων ·
title: Contra Arianos I.39: Made Man That We Might Be Made God
author: Athanasius of Alexandria

en: The soul that is deemed worthy to participate in the Spirit of his light, and is illuminated by the beauty of his ineffable glory, since he has prepared it for himself as a throne and dwelling—such a soul becomes all light, all face, all eye.
orig: ἡ ψυχὴ ἡ καταξιωθεῖσα τοῦ πνεύματος τοῦ φωτὸς αὐτοῦ μετασχεῖν καὶ καταλαμφθεῖσα ὑπὸ τοῦ κάλλους τῆς ἀρρήτου δόξης αὐτοῦ · θρόνον καὶ κατοικητήριον ἑαυτῷ κατασκευάσαντος · ὅλη φῶς γίνεται καὶ ὅλη πρόσωπον καὶ ὅλη ὀφθαλμός ·
title: Homily 15: All Light, All Face, All Eye
author: Pseudo-Macarius

en: There is no part of the soul that is not full of the spiritual eyes of light. That is to say, there is no part of it that is in darkness, but it has been made entirely light and spirit. It is wholly full of eyes and has no back part, but stands as a face in every direction, with the ineffable beauty of the glory of the light of Christ having mounted and seated itself upon it.
orig: οὐδὲν γάρ ἐστιν αὐτῆς μέρος ὃ μή ἐστιν πλῆρες τῶν πνευματικῶν ὀφθαλμῶν τοῦ φωτός · τουτέστιν οὐδὲν αὐτῆς μέρος ἐσκοτισμένον ἐστίν · ἀλλ’ ὅλη φῶς καὶ πνεῦμα γεγένηται ·
title: Homily 15: All Light, All Face, All Eye
author: Pseudo-Macarius

en: For God loved humankind, for whose sake he made the world, to whom he subjected all things that are on earth, to whom he gave reason and mind (logon kai noun, λόγον καὶ νοῦν), to whom alone he permitted to look up to him, whom he formed in his own image, to whom he sent his only-begotten Son, to whom he promised the kingdom in heaven—and he will give it to those who love him.
orig: ὁ γὰρ θεὸς τοὺς ἀνθρώπους ἠγάπησεν · δι’ οὓς ἐποίησε τὸν κόσμον · οἷς ὑπέταξε πάντα τὰ ἐν τῇ γῇ · οἷς λόγον καὶ νοῦν ἔδωκεν ·
title: Letter to Diognetus 10: Imitator of God's Goodness
author: Author of the Letter to Diognetus

en: And when you have acquired this knowledge, with what joy do you think you will be filled? Or how will you love him who so first loved you? And by loving him you will be an imitator of his goodness. And do not wonder that a human being can become an imitator of God—he can, since God wills it.
orig: ἓν δὲ ἐπιγνῷς ταύτην τὴν γνῶσιν · ποίας οἴει χαρᾶς πληρωθήσεσθαι · ἢ πῶς ἀγαπήσεις τὸν οὕτως προαγαπήσαντά σε · ἀγαπήσας δὲ μιμητὴς ἔσῃ τῆς χρηστότητος αὐτοῦ ·
title: Letter to Diognetus 10: Imitator of God's Goodness
author: Author of the Letter to Diognetus

en: For happiness does not consist in dominating one’s neighbours, nor in wishing to have more than the weak, nor in being rich and able to compel those who are beneath you. In these things no one can imitate God. But whoever takes upon himself the burden of his neighbour, whoever wishes to benefit one who is worse off in something in which he himself is better off, whoever provides to those in need what he received from God—this one becomes a god (theos, θεός) to those who receive from him. This one is an imitator of God.
orig: ὃς δ’ ἂν τὸ τοῦ πλησίον ἀναδέχηται βάρος · ὃς ἐν ἷ κρείσσων ἐστὶν ἕτερον τὸν ἐλαττούμενον εὐεργετεῖν θέλει · οὗτος θεὸς γίνεται τῶν λαμβανόντων · οὗτος μιμητὴς θεοῦ ἐστίν ·
title: Letter to Diognetus 10: Imitator of God's Goodness
author: Author of the Letter to Diognetus

en: So it is fitting that you should be in agreement with the mind of the bishop, which indeed you do. For your justly renowned council of elders, worthy of God, is attuned to the bishop as the strings to a lyre.
orig: ὅθεν πρέπει ὑμῖν συντρέχειν τῇ τοῦ ἐπισκόπου γνώμῃ · ὅπερ καὶ ποιεῖτε · τὸ γὰρ ἀξιόνομαστον ὑμῶν πρεσβυτέριον τοῦ θεοῦ ἄξιον οὕτως συνήρμοσται τῷ ἐπισκόπῳ ὡς χορδαὶ κιθάρᾳ ·
title: To the Ephesians 4: The Blood of God
author: Ignatius of Antioch

en: Therefore in your concord and harmonious love, Jesus Christ is sung. And each one of you must join this chorus, so that being harmonious in concord, receiving the pitch from God in unison, you may sing with one voice through Jesus Christ to the Father, that he may both hear you and recognise, through what you do well, that you are members of his Son.
orig: διὸ ἐν τῇ ὁμονοίᾳ ὑμῶν καὶ συμφώνῳ ἀγάπῃ Ιησοῦς Χριστὸς ἄδεται ·
title: To the Ephesians 4: The Blood of God
author: Ignatius of Antioch

en: Being as you are imitators of God, once you took on new life through the blood of God (en haimati theou, ἐν αἵματι θεοῦ) you perfectly accomplished the task natural to you.
orig: ἀναζωπυρήσαντες ἐν αἵματι θεοῦ · τὸ συγγενικὸν ἔργον τελείως ἀπηρτίσατε ·
title: To the Ephesians 4: The Blood of God
author: Ignatius of Antioch

en: Since, then, he renewed us by the remission of sins, he made us another type (typon, τύπον), so that we should have the soul of children, as if he were creating us afresh (anaplassontos, ἀναπλάσσοντος).
orig: ἐπεὶ οὖν ἀνακαινίσας ἡμᾶς ἐν τῇ ἀφέσει τῶν ἁμαρτιῶν ἐποίησεν ἡμᾶς ἄλλον τύπον · ὡς παιδίων ἔχειν τὴν ψυχήν · ὡς ἂν δὴ ἀναπλάσσοντος αὐτοῦ ἡμᾶς ·
title: Epistle of Barnabas 6.12: Creating Us Afresh
author: Author of the Epistle of Barnabas

en: For the scripture says about us, as he said to the Son: “Let us make humankind according to our image and according to our likeness, and let them rule over the beasts of the earth, and the birds of the heaven, and the fish of the sea.” And the Lord said, on seeing our good creation: “Increase and multiply and fill the earth.” These things he said to the Son.
orig: λέγει γὰρ ἡ γραφὴ περὶ ἡμῶν ὡς λέγει τῷ υἱῷ · ποιήσωμεν κατ’ εἰκόνα καὶ καθ’ ὁμοίωσιν ἡμῶν ἄνθρωπον ·
title: Epistle of Barnabas 6.12: Creating Us Afresh
author: Author of the Epistle of Barnabas

en: Again I will show you how the Lord speaks concerning us. He made a second creation (deuteran plasin, δευτέραν πλάσιν) in the last days. And the Lord says: “Behold, I make the last things as the first.” For this reason, then, the prophet proclaimed: “Enter into a land flowing with milk and honey, and rule over it.”
orig: πάλιν ἐπιδείξω σοι πῶς πρὸς ἡμᾶς λέγει ὁ κύριος · δευτέραν πλάσιν ἐπ’ ἐσχάτων ἐποίησεν · λέγει δὲ κύριος · ἰδοὺ ποιῶ τὰ ἔσχατα ὡς τὰ πρῶτα ·
title: Epistle of Barnabas 6.12: Creating Us Afresh
author: Author of the Epistle of Barnabas

en: There was neither being nor non-being then. There was no air, nor the sky beyond it. What covered it? Where was it? In whose protection? Was there water, fathomlessly deep?
orig: nāsad āsīn no sad āsīt tadānīṃ nāsīd rajo no vyomā paro yat | kim āvarīvaḥ kuha kasya śarmann ambhaḥ kim āsīd gahanaṃ gabhīram
title: Rig Veda 10.129: The Hymn of Creation
author: Vedic Rishis

en: There was no death then, nor immortality. There was no sign of night or day. That One breathed, without breath, by its own impulse. Other than that, there was nothing at all.
orig: na mṛtyur āsīd amṛtaṃ na tarhi na rātryā ahna āsīt praketāḥ | ānīd avātaṃ svadhāyā tad ekaṃ tasmād dhānyan na paraḥ kiṃ canāsa
title: Rig Veda 10.129: The Hymn of Creation
author: Vedic Rishis

en: Darkness was hidden by darkness in the beginning. All this was an undistinguished sea. That which was covered by the void, That One, was born through the power of heat (tapas).
orig: tama āsīt tamasā gūḷham agre ‘prakeataṃ salilaṃ sarvam ā idam | tucchyenābhv apihitaṃ yad āsīt tapasas tan mahinājāyataikam
title: Rig Veda 10.129: The Hymn of Creation
author: Vedic Rishis

en: Desire (kama) arose in the beginning, the first seed of mind. Poets, searching in their hearts with wisdom, found the bond of being in non-being.
orig: kāmas tad agre sam avartataādhi manaso retaḥ prathamaṃ yad āsīt | sato bandham asati nir avindan hṛdi pratīṣyā kavayo manīṣā
title: Rig Veda 10.129: The Hymn of Creation
author: Vedic Rishis

en: Their cord was stretched across. Was there a below? Was there an above? There were seed-bearers, there were powers. There was impulse from below, there was giving forth from above.
orig: tiraścīno vitato raśmir eṣām adhaḥ svid āsīd upari svid āsīt | retodhā āsan mahimāna āsan svadhā avastāt prayatiḥ parastāt
title: Rig Veda 10.129: The Hymn of Creation
author: Vedic Rishis

en: Who really knows? Who here can say? When was it born? From where did this creation come? The gods came afterwards, with the creation of this world. So who knows from where it arose?
orig: ko addhā veda ka iha pra vocat kuta ājātā kuta iyaṃ visṛṣṭiḥ | arvāg devā asya visarjanena athā ko veda yata ābabhūva
title: Rig Veda 10.129: The Hymn of Creation
author: Vedic Rishis

en: From where this creation arose—whether he made it or did not—he who surveys it from the highest heaven, he alone knows. Or perhaps even he does not know.
orig: iyaṃ visṛṣṭir yata ābabhūva yadi vā dadhe yadi vā na | yo asyādhyakṣaḥ parame vyoman so aṅga veda yadi vā na veda
title: Rig Veda 10.129: The Hymn of Creation
author: Vedic Rishis

en: The Purusha (cosmic person) has a thousand heads, a thousand eyes, a thousand feet. He pervades the earth on every side and extends ten fingers’ breadth beyond it.
orig: sahasrāśīrṣā puruṣaḥ sahasrākṣaḥ sahasrapāt | sa bhūmiṃ viśvato vṛtvātyatiṣṭhad daśāṅgulam
title: Rig Veda 10.90: Purusha Sukta (The Cosmic Person)
author: Vedic Rishis

en: Purusha is all this—what has been and what will be. He is the lord of immortality, which he outgrows through food.
orig: puruṣa eved aṃ sarvaṃ yad bhūtaṃ yac ca bhavyam | utāmṛtatvasyēśāno yad annenātirohati
title: Rig Veda 10.90: Purusha Sukta (The Cosmic Person)
author: Vedic Rishis

en: Such is his greatness, and yet Purusha is greater still. One quarter of him is all beings. Three quarters are the immortal in heaven.
orig: etāvānasya mahimā ato jyāyāṃś ca pūruṣaḥ | pādo’sya viśvā bhūtāni tripādasyāmṛtaṃ divi
title: Rig Veda 10.90: Purusha Sukta (The Cosmic Person)
author: Vedic Rishis

en: Three quarters of Purusha rose upward. One quarter remained here. From this he spread in all directions, into what eats and what does not eat.
orig: tripād ūrdhva ud ait puruṣaḥ pādo’syēhābh avat punaḥ | tato viṣvaṅ vy akrāmat sāśanānaśane abhi
title: Rig Veda 10.90: Purusha Sukta (The Cosmic Person)
author: Vedic Rishis

en: From him Viraj was born, and from Viraj came Purusha. As soon as he was born, he extended beyond the earth, both behind and in front. When the gods performed the sacrifice with Purusha as the offering, spring was its clarified butter, summer the fuel, and autumn the oblation.
orig: tasmād virāḷ ajāyata virājo adhi pūruṣaḥ | sa jāto aty aricyata paścād bhūmim atho puraḥ | yat puruṣeṇa haviṣā devā yajñam atanvata | vasanto asyāsīd ājyaṃ grīṣma idhśmaḥ śarad dhaviḥ
title: Rig Veda 10.90: Purusha Sukta (The Cosmic Person)
author: Vedic Rishis

en: When they divided Purusha, into how many parts did they apportion him? What was his mouth? What were his arms? What were his thighs and feet called? The Brahmin was his mouth, the Rajanya (warrior) was made his arms. His thighs became the Vaishya (merchant), from his feet the Shudra (servant) was born.
orig: yat puruṣaṃ vy adadhuḥ katidhā vy akalpayan | mukhaṃ kim asya kau bāhū kā ūrū pādā ucyete | brāhmaṇo’sya mukham āsīd bāhū rājanyaḥ kṛtaḥ | ūrū tad asya yad vaiśyaḥ padabhyāṃ śūdro ajāyata
title: Rig Veda 10.90: Purusha Sukta (The Cosmic Person)
author: Vedic Rishis

en: The moon was born from his mind. From his eye the sun was born. From his mouth Indra and Agni, from his breath Vayu (the wind) was born. From his navel came the atmosphere. From his head the sky evolved. From his feet the earth, from his ear the directions. Thus they fashioned the worlds.
orig: candramā manaso jātaś cakṣoḥ sūryo ajāyata | mukhād indraś cāgniś ca prāṇād vāyur ajāyata | nābhyā āsīd antarikṣaṃ śīrṣṇo dyauḥ sam avartata | padabhyāṃ bhūmir diśaḥ śrotrāt tathā lokā̅āṃ akalpayan
title: Rig Veda 10.90: Purusha Sukta (The Cosmic Person)
author: Vedic Rishis

en: With the sacrifice the gods sacrificed the sacrifice. These were the first laws. These powers reached the firmament, where the ancient gods and perfected beings dwell.
orig: yajñen a yajñam ayajanta devās tāni dharmāṇi prathamāny āsan | te ha nākaṃ mahimānaḥ sacanta yatra pūrve sādhyāḥ santi devāḥ
title: Rig Veda 10.90: Purusha Sukta (The Cosmic Person)
author: Vedic Rishis

en: Uddalaka Aruni said to his son Shvetaketu: “Learn from me, dear one, the nature of sleep. When a person sleeps, as it is called, then he has merged with Being (sat). He has gone to his own. That is why they say he sleeps (svapiti), for he has gone to his own (svam apītaḥ).”
orig: uddālako hāruṇiḥ śvetaketuṃ putram uvāca svapiteti ha somya vijajānīhiti yadā puruṣaḥ svapiteti ācakṣate satā somya tadā sampanno bhavati svam apīto bhavati tasmād enaṃ svapitety ācakṣate svam hy apīto bhavati
title: Chandogya Upanishad 6.8-16: Tat Tvam Asi (You Are That)
author: Upanishadic Sages

en: “As a bird tied by a string, after flying in every direction without finding rest elsewhere, settles down at the place where it is bound—so too, dear one, the mind, after flying in every direction without finding rest elsewhere, settles down in breath (prāṇa), for the mind is bound to breath.”
orig: sa yathā śakuniḥ sūtreṇa prabaddhaḥ diśaṃ diśam apatītvānyatrāyatanam alab dhvā bandhanam evōpaśrayata evam eva khalu somya tan mano diśaṃ diśam apatītvānyatrāyatanam alabdhvā prāṇam evōpaśrayate prāṇabandhanaṃ hi somya mana iti
title: Chandogya Upanishad 6.8-16: Tat Tvam Asi (You Are That)
author: Upanishadic Sages

en: “Now, that which is the finest essence—this whole world has that as its Self. That is the True. That is the Self. You are That (tat tvam asi), Shvetaketu.”
orig: sa ya eṣo’ṇimāitad ātmyam idaṃ sarvam tat satyam sa ātmā tat tvam asi śvetaketo iti
title: Chandogya Upanishad 6.8-16: Tat Tvam Asi (You Are That)
author: Upanishadic Sages

en: “Bring me a fruit of that banyan tree.” “Here it is, sir.” “Break it.” “It is broken, sir.” “What do you see there?” “These tiny seeds, sir.” “Break one of them.” “It is broken, sir.” “What do you see there?” “Nothing at all, sir.” He said: “That finest essence which you do not perceive—from that very essence this great banyan tree stands. Believe it, dear one. That which is the finest essence—this whole world has that as its Self. That is the True. That is the Self. You are That, Shvetaketu.”
orig: nyagrodhaph alam ata āhāreti idam bhagava iti bhinddhīti bhinnam bhagava iti kim atr a paśyasi iti aṇvya itvā bha gava iti tāsām aṅga ekāṃ bhinddhīti bhinnā bhagava iti kim atra paśyasi iti na kiṃcana bhagava iti | tam hovāca yaṃ vai somyait am aṇimānam na nibhālayase etasmād vā are’ṇim na etasmād evāyaṃ mahany agrodhas tiṣṭhati śraddhasva somya iti sa ya eṣo’ṇimāitad ātmyam idaṃ sarvam tat satyam sa ātmā tat tvam asi śvetaketo iti
title: Chandogya Upanishad 6.8-16: Tat Tvam Asi (You Are That)
author: Upanishadic Sages

en: “Place this salt in water, and come to me in the morning.” He did so. The father said: “Bring me the salt you placed in the water last night.” He looked for it but could not find it, for it had dissolved. “Sip from this end. How is it?” “Salty.” “Sip from the middle. How is it?” “Salty.” “Sip from the far end. How is it?” “Salty.” “Throw it away and come to me.” He could not find the salt, but it was always there. “That finest essence which you do not perceive—that is the Self of this whole world. That is the True. That is the Self. You are That, Shvetaketu.”
orig: lavaṇam etad udake’v adhāyātha mā prātar upasīdetīti sa ha tathā cakāra tam hovāca yad doṣā lavaṇam udake’vadhithā aṅga tad āhāreti tad dhāvamṛśya na viveda yathā vīliīnam evāsyāgraād ācāmeti katham iti lavaṇam iti madhyat ācāmeti katham iti lavaṇam iti antād ācāmeti katham iti lavaṇam iti | sa ya eṣo’ṇimāitad ātmyam idaṃ sarvam tat satyam sa ātmā tat tvam asi śvetaketo iti
title: Chandogya Upanishad 6.8-16: Tat Tvam Asi (You Are That)
author: Upanishadic Sages

en: “Just as a man from Gandhara, blindfolded and led away, might be left in a deserted place, and just as he would call out in every direction, ‘I have been brought here blindfolded, I have been left here blindfolded!’—and just as someone might remove his blindfold and say, ‘Gandhara is in that direction; go that way,’ and he, asking his way from village to village, would finally arrive home—so too a person who has a teacher knows: ‘I shall remain in this world only until I am liberated. Then I shall reach home.’ That which is the finest essence—this whole world has that as its Self. That is the True. That is the Self. You are That, Shvetaketu.”
orig: yathā somya puruṣaṃ gandhārebhyo’bhina ddham ānayaṃ taṃ tato’tij ane vi sṛjet sa yathā tatra prāṅ vōdaṅ vā pratyaṅ vā udancam iti abhinaddho’bhip raṇīto’bhijanaṃ visṛṣṭaḥ | tasya yathābhina hanaṃ pramu cya pravṛjeta taṃ sa evam eva somya vijanavatpuruṣo vid vān | sa ya eṣo’ṇimāitad ātmyam idaṃ sarvam tat satyam sa ātmā tat tvam asi śvetaketo iti
title: Chandogya Upanishad 6.8-16: Tat Tvam Asi (You Are That)
author: Upanishadic Sages

en: “That which is the finest essence—this whole world has that as its Self. That is the True. That is the Self. You are That, Shvetaketu.” “Please, sir, teach me more.” “So be it, dear one,” he said.
orig: sa ya eṣo’ṇimāitad ātmyam idaṃ sarvam tat satyam sa ātmā tat tvam asi śvetaketo iti bhūya eva mā bhagavān vijñāpayatv iti tathā somyeti hovāca
title: Chandogya Upanishad 6.8-16: Tat Tvam Asi (You Are That)
author: Upanishadic Sages

en: In the beginning, this world was the Self (atman) alone, in the form of a person. Looking around, he saw nothing other than himself. He first said: “I am” (aham asmi). From that the name “I” (aham) arose. Therefore even today, when addressed, a person first says “It is I,” and then speaks whatever other name he has.
orig: ātmā vā idam eka evāgra āsīt puruṣavidhaḥ so’nuvekṣya nānyad ātmano’paśyat so’ham asmīty agre vyāharat tato’haṃnāmābhavat tasmād apy etarhy āmantraṇaḥ puruṣo’ham ayam ity evagre uktvāthānyan nāma prabrute yad asya bhavati
title: Brihadaranyaka Upanishad 1.4: Aham Brahmasmi (I Am Brahman)
author: Upanishadic Sages

en: He was afraid. Therefore one who is alone is afraid. But he thought: “Since there is nothing other than myself, of what am I afraid?” Then his fear departed. For what should he have feared? One fears only a second.
orig: so’bibhet tasmād ekākī bibheti sa hayaṃ aīkṣata yan mad anyan nāsti kasmān nu bibhemīti tata evāsya bhayaṃ vīyayāya kasmād dhy abheṣyad dvitīyād vai bhayaṃ bhavati
title: Brihadaranyaka Upanishad 1.4: Aham Brahmasmi (I Am Brahman)
author: Upanishadic Sages

en: He had no delight. Therefore one who is alone has no delight. He desired a second. He was as large as a man and woman in close embrace. He caused that Self to fall into two pieces. From that arose husband and wife. Therefore, as Yajnavalkya used to say, this body is one half of oneself, like one half of a split pea. Therefore this space is filled by a wife.
orig: sa vai naiva reme tasmād ekākī na ramate sa dvitīyam aicchat sa haitāvān āsa yathā strīpumāṃsau sampariṣvaktau sa imam evātmānaṃ dvaidhāpātayat tataḥ patiś ca patnī cābhavatām tasmād idam ardhabṛgalam iva svaḥ iti ha smāha yājñavalkyas tasmād ayaṃ ākāśaḥ striyā pūryata eva
title: Brihadaranyaka Upanishad 1.4: Aham Brahmasmi (I Am Brahman)
author: Upanishadic Sages

en: Brahman was this in the beginning. It knew only itself: “I am Brahman” (aham brahmāsmi). Therefore it became all. And whoever among the gods awakened to this also became That. The same is true among sages and among human beings. Seeing this, the sage Vamadeva declared: “I was Manu and the sun.” Even now, whoever knows “I am Brahman” becomes this whole world. Even the gods cannot prevent it, for he becomes their very Self.
orig: brahma vā idam agra āsīt tad ātmānam evāvet aham brahmāsmīti tasmāt tat sarvam abhavat tad yo yo devānāṃ pratyaābudhyata sa eva tad abhavat tathārṣīṇāṃ tathā manuṣyāṇāṃ tad dhaitat paśyan ṛṣir vāmadevaḥ pratipede’haṃ manur abh avaṃ sūryaś ceti tad idam apy etarhi ya evaṃ vedaāhaṃ brahmāsmīti sa idaṃ sarvaṃ bhavati tasya ha na devāś canābhūtyā īśate ātmā hy eṣāṃ sa bhavati
title: Brihadaranyaka Upanishad 1.4: Aham Brahmasmi (I Am Brahman)
author: Upanishadic Sages

en: AUM. This syllable is this whole world. Its explanation is as follows: the past, the present, the future—everything is the sound AUM. And whatever else there is beyond the three times—that too is the sound AUM.
orig: oṃ ity etad akṣaram idaṃ sarvam tasyopavykhyanam bhūtam bhavad bhaviṣyad iti sarvam oṃkāra eva yac cānyat trikālātītam tad apy oṃkāra eva
title: Mandukya Upanishad: AUM and the Four States
author: Upanishadic Sages

en: All this is Brahman. This Self (atman) is Brahman. This Self has four quarters.
orig: sarvam hy etad brahma ayam ātmā brahma so’yam ātmā catuṣpāt
title: Mandukya Upanishad: AUM and the Four States
author: Upanishadic Sages

en: The first quarter is Vaishvanara, the waking state, whose field is the external world, who has seven limbs and nineteen mouths, and who experiences gross objects.
orig: jāgaritasthāno bahiprājñaḥ saptanga ekonaviṃśatimukhaḥ sthūlabhug vaiśvānaraḥ prathamaḥ pādaḥ
title: Mandukya Upanishad: AUM and the Four States
author: Upanishadic Sages

en: The second quarter is Taijasa, the dreaming state, whose field is the internal world, who has seven limbs and nineteen mouths, and who experiences subtle objects.
orig: svapnasthāno’ntaḥprājñaḥ saptanga ekonaviṃśatimukhaḥ praviviktabhuk taijaso dvitīyaḥ pādaḥ
title: Mandukya Upanishad: AUM and the Four States
author: Upanishadic Sages

en: Where the sleeper desires no desires and sees no dreams—that is deep sleep. The third quarter is Prajna, the deep sleep state, who has become one, who is a mass of consciousness, who is full of bliss, who truly enjoys bliss, whose mouth is thought.
orig: yatra supto na kaṃcana kāmaṃ kāmayate na kaṃcana svapnaṃ paśyati tat suṣuptam suṣuptasthāna ekībhūtaḥ prajñānaghana evānandamayo hy ānandabhuk ceto mukhaḥ prājñas tṛtīyaḥ pādaḥ
title: Mandukya Upanishad: AUM and the Four States
author: Upanishadic Sages

en: This is the lord of all. This is the knower of all. This is the inner controller. This is the source of all, for it is the origin and the end of all beings.
orig: eṣa sarveśvara eṣa sarvajña eṣo’ntaryāmy eṣa yoniḥ sarvasya prabhavāpyayau hi bhūtānām
title: Mandukya Upanishad: AUM and the Four States
author: Upanishadic Sages

en: Not inwardly cognitive, not outwardly cognitive, not cognitive in both ways, not a mass of cognition, not cognitive, not non-cognitive. Unseen, beyond all dealings, ungraspable, without distinguishing marks, unthinkable, indescribable, its essence the firm conviction of the oneness of itself, the cessation of the manifold world, peaceful, auspicious, without a second—that is the fourth (turiya). That is the Self. That is to be known.
orig: nāntaḥprajñaṃ na bahiprājñaṃ nobhayataḥprājñaṃ na prajñānaghanaṃ na prajñaṃ nāprajñam adṛṣṭam avyavahāryam agrāhyam alakṣaṇam acintyam avyapadeśyam ekātmapratyayasarāṃ prapañcopaśamaṃ śāntam śivam advaitam caturthaṃ manyante sa ātmā sa vijñeyaḥ
title: Mandukya Upanishad: AUM and the Four States
author: Upanishadic Sages

en: That same Self, considered from the standpoint of the syllable, is AUM. Considered from the standpoint of the letters, the quarters of the Self are the letters of AUM, and the letters are the quarters: A, U, M.
orig: so’yam ātmādhyakṣaram oṃkāro’dhimātraṃ pādā mātrā mātrāś ca pādā akāra ukāro makāra iti
title: Mandukya Upanishad: AUM and the Four States
author: Upanishadic Sages

en: The waking state, Vaishvanara, is the letter A, the first element, from apti (obtaining) or from adimatva (being first). He who knows this obtains all desires and becomes first.
orig: jāgaritasthāno vaiśvānaro’kāraḥ prathamā mātrāpter ādimatvad vāpnoti ha vai sarvān kāmān ādiś ca bhavati ya evaṃ veda
title: Mandukya Upanishad: AUM and the Four States
author: Upanishadic Sages

en: The dreaming state, Taijasa, is the letter U, the second element, from utkarsha (exaltation) or from ubhayatva (being in between). He who knows this exalts the stream of knowledge and becomes equal. In his family, no one ignorant of Brahman will be born.
orig: svapnasthānas taijasa ukāro dvitīyā mātrōtkarṣād ubhayatvād vōtkarṣati ha vai jñānasantatim samānaś ca bhavati nāsyābrahmavit kule bhavati ya evaṃ veda
title: Mandukya Upanishad: AUM and the Four States
author: Upanishadic Sages

en: The deep sleep state, Prajna, is the letter M, the third element, from miti (measuring) or from apiti (merging). He who knows this measures all this and becomes the place into which all merges.
orig: suṣuptasthānaḥ prājño makāras tṛtīyā mātrā miter apīter vā minoti ha vā idaṃ sarvam apītiś ca bhavati ya evaṃ veda
title: Mandukya Upanishad: AUM and the Four States
author: Upanishadic Sages

en: The fourth (turiya) is without a letter. It is beyond all dealings, the cessation of the manifold world, auspicious, without a second. Thus AUM is the Self indeed. He who knows this enters the Self through the Self.
orig: amātraś caturthaḥ avyavahāryaḥ prapañcopaśamaṃ śivam advaitam evam oṃkāra ātmaiva saṃviśaty ātmānam ātmanā ya evaṃ veda ya evaṃ veda
title: Mandukya Upanishad: AUM and the Four States
author: Upanishadic Sages

en: All this—whatever moves in this moving world—is pervaded by the Lord. Therefore find your enjoyment in renunciation. Do not covet the wealth of anyone.
orig: īśā vāsyam idaṃ sarvaṃ yat kiṃ ca jagatyaṃ jagat | tena tyaktena bhuñjīthā mā gṛdhaḥ kasya svid dhanam
title: Isha Upanishad 1-3: The Self Is Everywhere
author: Upanishadic Sages

en: By performing action in this world, one should wish to live a hundred years. Thus, and in no other way, action does not cling to you.
orig: kurvann eveh a karmāṇi jijīviṣec chataṃ samāḥ | evaṃ tvayi nānyathet o’sti na karma lipyate nare
title: Isha Upanishad 1-3: The Self Is Everywhere
author: Upanishadic Sages

en: Those worlds, shrouded in blind darkness, are called “sunless.” To them, after death, go those who slay the Self.
orig: asuryā nāma te lokā andhena tamasāvṛtāḥ | tāṃs te pretyabhigacchanti ye ke cātmahano janāḥ
title: Isha Upanishad 1-3: The Self Is Everywhere
author: Upanishadic Sages

en: The Self (atman), smaller than the small, greater than the great, is hidden in the heart of every creature. A person who is free from desire, with mind and senses composed, beholds the majesty of the Self and becomes free from sorrow.
orig: aṇor aṇīyān mahato mahīyān ātmāsya jantor nih ito guhāyām | tam akratuḥ paśyati vītasoko dhātuḥ prasādān mahimānam ātmanaḥ
title: Katha Upanishad 2.20: The Self in the Heart
author: Upanishadic Sages

en: Sitting, he travels far. Lying down, he goes everywhere. Who other than me can know this god who rejoices and does not rejoice?
orig: āsīno dūraṃ vrajati śayāno yāti sarvataḥ | kas taṃ madamādaṃ devaṃ mad anyo jñātum arhati
title: Katha Upanishad 2.20: The Self in the Heart
author: Upanishadic Sages

en: The wise one who knows the Self as bodiless among bodies, as permanent among the impermanent, as great and all-pervading—he does not grieve.
orig: aśarīraṃ śarīreṣv anavaastheṣv avasthitam | mahantaṃ vibhum ātmānaṃ matvā dhīro na śocati
title: Katha Upanishad 2.20: The Self in the Heart
author: Upanishadic Sages

en: This Self cannot be attained by instruction, nor by intellect, nor by much learning. It is attained only by the one whom it chooses. To such a one, the Self reveals its own form.
orig: nāyam ātmā pravacanena labhyo na medhayā na bahunā śrutena | yam evaiṣa vṛṇute tena labhyas tasyaiṣa ātmā vivṛṇute tanuṃ svām
title: Katha Upanishad 2.20: The Self in the Heart
author: Upanishadic Sages

en: Know that to be indestructible by which all this is pervaded. No one can bring about the destruction of this imperishable reality.
orig: avināśi tu tad viddhi yena sarvam idaṃ tatam | vināśam avyayasyāsya na kaścit kartum arhati
title: Bhagavad Gita 2.17-20: The Eternal Self
author: Vyasa (attributed)

en: These bodies of the embodied Self, which is eternal, indestructible, and immeasurable, are said to have an end. Therefore fight, O Bharata.
orig: antavanta ime dehā nityasyoktāḥ śarīriṇaḥ | anāśino’prameyasya tasmād yudhyasva bhārata
title: Bhagavad Gita 2.17-20: The Eternal Self
author: Vyasa (attributed)

en: He who thinks the Self is the slayer and he who thinks it is slain—both are ignorant. It does not slay, nor is it slain.
orig: ya enaṃ vetti hantāraṃ yaś cainaṃ manyate hatam | ubhau tau na vijānīto nāyaṃ hanti na hanyate
title: Bhagavad Gita 2.17-20: The Eternal Self
author: Vyasa (attributed)

en: It is not born, nor does it ever die. Having been, it will never cease to be. Unborn, eternal, permanent, and primeval, it is not slain when the body is slain.
orig: na jāyate mriyate vā kadācin nāyaṃ bhūtvā bhavitā vā na bhūyaḥ | ajo nityaḥ śāśvato’yaṃ purāṇo na hanyate hanyamāne śarīre
title: Bhagavad Gita 2.17-20: The Eternal Self
author: Vyasa (attributed)

en: Whenever there is a decline of dharma (righteousness) and a rise of adharma (unrighteousness), O Bharata, then I manifest myself.
orig: yadā yadā hi dharmasya glānir bhavati bhārata | abhyutthānam adharmasya tadātmānaṃ sṛjāmy aham
title: Bhagavad Gita 4.7-8: Whenever Dharma Declines
author: Vyasa (attributed)

en: For the protection of the good, for the destruction of the wicked, and for the establishment of dharma, I come into being age after age.
orig: paritrāṇāya sādhūnāṃ vināśāya ca duṣkṛtām | dharmasamsthāpanārthāya sambhavāmi yuge yuge
title: Bhagavad Gita 4.7-8: Whenever Dharma Declines
author: Vyasa (attributed)

en: I am the Self, O Gudakesha (Arjuna), seated in the heart of all beings. I am the beginning, the middle, and the end of all beings.
orig: aham ātmā guḍākeśa sarvabhūtāśayasthitaḥ | aham ādiś ca madhyaṃ ca bhūtānām anta eva ca
title: Bhagavad Gita 10.20: I Am the Self in the Heart of All
author: Vyasa (attributed)

en: Among the Adityas, I am Vishnu. Among lights, I am the radiant sun. Among the Maruts, I am Marichi. Among the stars, I am the moon. Among the Vedas, I am the Sama Veda. Among the gods, I am Indra. Among the senses, I am the mind. In beings, I am consciousness.
orig: ādityānām ahaṃ viṣṇur jyotiṣāṃ ravir aṃśumān | marīcir marutām asmi nakṣatrāṇām ahaṃ śaśī | vedānāṃ sāmavedo’smi devānām asmi vāsavaḥ | indriyāṇāṃ manaś cāsmi bhūtānām asmi cetanā
title: Bhagavad Gita 10.20: I Am the Self in the Heart of All
author: Vyasa (attributed)

en: And whatever is the seed of all beings, that I am, O Arjuna. There is no being, moving or unmoving, that can exist without me.
orig: yac cāpi sarvabhūtānāṃ bījaṃ tad aham arjuna | na tad asti vinā yat syān mayā bhūtaṃ carācaram
title: Bhagavad Gita 10.20: I Am the Self in the Heart of All
author: Vyasa (attributed)

en: Whatever being is glorious, prosperous, or powerful, know that it springs from a fraction of my splendour. But what need is there for all this detail, Arjuna? I stand sustaining this entire world with a single fragment of myself.
orig: yad yad vibhūtimat sattvaṃ śrīmad ūrjitam eva vā | tat tad evāvagaccha tvaṃ mama tejo’ṃśasambhavam | athavā bahunaitena kiṃ jñātena tavārjuna | viṣṭabhyaāham idaṃ kṛtsnam ekāṃśena sthito jagat
title: Bhagavad Gita 10.20: I Am the Self in the Heart of All
author: Vyasa (attributed)

en: Fix your mind on me, be devoted to me, sacrifice to me, bow down to me. You shall come to me. Truly I promise you: you are dear to me.
orig: manmanā bhava madbhakto madyājī māṃ namaskuru | mām evaiṣyasi satyaṃ te pratijāne priyo’si me
title: Bhagavad Gita 18.65-66: Surrender to Me Alone
author: Vyasa (attributed)

en: Abandoning all dharmas (duties), take refuge in me alone. I shall liberate you from all sins. Do not grieve.
orig: sarvadharman parityajya mām ekaṃ śaraṇaṃ vraja | ahaṃ tvā sarvapāpebhyo mokṣayiṣyāmi mā śucaḥ
title: Bhagavad Gita 18.65-66: Surrender to Me Alone
author: Vyasa (attributed)

en: Now, the teaching of yoga.
orig: atha yogānuśāsanam
title: Yoga Sutras 1.1-4: Yoga Is the Stilling of the Mind
author: Patanjali

en: Yoga is the stilling of the fluctuations of the mind.
orig: yogaś citta-vṛtti-nirodhaḥ
title: Yoga Sutras 1.1-4: Yoga Is the Stilling of the Mind
author: Patanjali

en: Then the seer abides in its own true nature.
orig: tadā draṣṭuḥ svarūpe’vasthānam
title: Yoga Sutras 1.1-4: Yoga Is the Stilling of the Mind
author: Patanjali

en: Otherwise, the seer conforms to the fluctuations [of the mind].
orig: vṛtti-sārūpyam itaratra
title: Yoga Sutras 1.1-4: Yoga Is the Stilling of the Mind
author: Patanjali

en: Mind is the forerunner of all actions. Mind is their chief; they are all mind-wrought. If with an impure mind a person speaks or acts, suffering follows him like the wheel that follows the foot of the ox.
orig: Manopubbangamā dhammā, manoseṭṭhā manomayā; manasā ce paduṭṭhena, bhāsati vā karoti vā, tato naṃ dukkhamanveti, cakkaṃ va vahato padaṃ.
title: Dhammapada 1-5: Mind Is the Forerunner
author: Gautama Buddha (attributed)

en: Mind is the forerunner of all actions. Mind is their chief; they are all mind-wrought. If with a pure mind a person speaks or acts, happiness follows him like his never-departing shadow.
orig: Manopubbangamā dhammā, manoseṭṭhā manomayā; manasā ce pasannena, bhāsati vā karoti vā, tato naṃ sukhamanveti, chāyā va anapayini.
title: Dhammapada 1-5: Mind Is the Forerunner
author: Gautama Buddha (attributed)

en: “He abused me, he struck me, he overpowered me, he robbed me.” Those who harbour such thoughts do not still their hatred.
orig: Akkocchi maṃ avadhi maṃ, ajīni maṃ ahāsi me; ye ca taṃ upanayhanti, veraṃ tesaṃ na sammati.
title: Dhammapada 1-5: Mind Is the Forerunner
author: Gautama Buddha (attributed)

en: “He abused me, he struck me, he overpowered me, he robbed me.” Those who do not harbour such thoughts still their hatred.
orig: Akkocchi maṃ avadhi maṃ, ajīni maṃ ahāsi me; ye ca taṃ nupanayhanti, veraṃ tesūpasammati.
title: Dhammapada 1-5: Mind Is the Forerunner
author: Gautama Buddha (attributed)

en: Hatred is never appeased by hatred in this world. By non-hatred alone is hatred appeased. This is a law eternal.
orig: Na hi verena verāni, sammantīdha kudācanaṃ; averena ca sammanti, esa dhammo sanantano.
title: Dhammapada 1-5: Mind Is the Forerunner
author: Gautama Buddha (attributed)

en: The noble Avalokiteśvara Bodhisattva, while practising the deep practice of the Perfection of Wisdom, looked upon the five aggregates and saw that in their own nature they were empty.
orig: Ārya-Avalokiteśvaro bodhisattvo gambhīrāṃ prajñāpāramitā-caryāṃ caramāṇo vyavalokayati sma: pañca-skandhās tāṃś ca svabhāva-śūnyān paśyati sma.
title: Heart Sutra: Form Is Emptiness
author: Nagarjuna (attributed)

en: “Śāriputra, form is not different from emptiness; emptiness is not different from form. Form itself is emptiness; emptiness itself is form. So too are feeling, perception, mental formations, and consciousness.”
orig: Iha Śāriputra rūpaṃ śūnyatā, śūnyataiva rūpam; rūpān na pṛthak śūnyatā, śūnyatāyā na pṛthag rūpam; yad rūpaṃ sā śūnyatā, yā śūnyatā tad rūpam. Evam eva vedanā-saṃjñā-saṃskāra-vijñānāni.
title: Heart Sutra: Form Is Emptiness
author: Nagarjuna (attributed)

en: “Śāriputra, all dharmas are marked with emptiness: they are neither produced nor destroyed, neither defiled nor pure, neither deficient nor complete. Therefore, in emptiness there is no form, no feeling, no perception, no mental formations, no consciousness.”
orig: Śāriputra sarva-dharmāḥ śūnyatā-lakṣaṇā, anutpannā aniruddhā, amalā avimalā, anūnā aparipūrṇāḥ. Tasmāc Chāriputra śūnyatāyāṃ na rūpaṃ, na vedanā, na saṃjñā, na saṃskārāḥ, na vijñānam.
title: Heart Sutra: Form Is Emptiness
author: Nagarjuna (attributed)

en: “There is no ignorance, no extinction of ignorance, and so forth up to no ageing and death, no extinction of ageing and death. There is no suffering, no origination, no cessation, no path. There is no wisdom, no attainment, and no non-attainment.”
orig: Na avidyā nāvidyā-kṣayo, yāvan na jarā-maraṇaṃ na jarā-maraṇa-kṣayo; na duḥkha-samudaya-nirodha-mārgā; na jñānaṃ, na prāptir na aprāptiḥ.
title: Heart Sutra: Form Is Emptiness
author: Nagarjuna (attributed)

en: Therefore, know the Perfection of Wisdom is the great mantra, the mantra of great knowledge, the unsurpassed mantra, the unequalled mantra, which removes all suffering. It is true, not false. So proclaim the Perfection of Wisdom mantra. Proclaim the mantra that says: gone, gone, gone beyond, gone utterly beyond—awakening!
orig: Tasmāj jñātavyaṃ prajñāpāramitā mahā-mantro mahā-vidyā-mantro ’nuttara-mantro ’samasama-mantraḥ, sarva-duḥkha-praśamanaḥ, satyam amithyatvāt. Prajñāpāramitāyām ukto mantraḥ. Tadyathā: gate gate pāragate pārasaṃgate bodhi svāhā.
title: Heart Sutra: Form Is Emptiness
author: Nagarjuna (attributed)

s: The Simile of the Withered Lotus
en: It is as if there were a great store of honey hidden in a cave, surrounded by ten thousand bees. A skilled person, seeing the honey, would drive away the bees and take the honey. Just so, O noble sons, all beings possess the Tathāgatagarbha. Like the honey surrounded by bees, it is always present, complete, and unchanged, but it is covered over by the defilements of ignorance and craving.
orig: Tadyathāpi nāma kulaputrāḥ madhukara-kośaḥ pratividdhaḥ daśabhir madhukara-sahasraiḥ. Kuśalo puruṣaḥ madhukaṃ dṛṣṭvā madhukarakān apāsya madhukaṃ gṛhṇīyāt. Evam eva kulaputrāḥ sarva-sattvānāṃ tathāgatagarbho ’sti. Yathā tad madhukaṃ madhukara-parivr̩tam, tathā sarva-sattvānāṃ tathāgatagarbhaḥ kleśāvidyā-parivr̩taḥ.
title: Tathagatagarbha Sutra: Buddha-Nature in All Beings
author: Gautama Buddha (attributed)

s: The Simile of the Gold in Filth
en: It is as if a lump of pure gold were to fall into a place of filth and defilement, where it might remain for many years without deteriorating. A person with divine sight might see it there and say to someone: “There is a lump of gold here in this filth. Dig it out and do with it as you please.” Just so, the Tathāgatagarbha of all beings is covered over by the defilements of craving, hatred, and delusion.
orig: Tadyathāpi nāma kulaputrāḥ kāñcanam aśucau patitaṃ varṣa-śatair api na viśīryate. Divya-cakṣuḥ kaścit puruṣaḥ paśyed vadec ca: atra kāñcanam asti, uddharatāsmat praśodhyāsya. Evam eva kulaputrāḥ sarva-sattvānāṃ tathāgatagarbho rāga-dveṣa-moha-kleśaiś channo bhavati.
title: Tathagatagarbha Sutra: Buddha-Nature in All Beings
author: Gautama Buddha (attributed)

en: All beings, though they find themselves in all sorts of circumstances among the destinies of the six realms of existence, possess the Tathāgatagarbha eternally and without change. This, O noble sons, is what is called the Tathāgatagarbha.
orig: Sarva-sattvāḥ ṣaḍ-gati-samsāra-gatā api tathāgatagarbha-samanvitāḥ nityam avipariṇāma-dharmiṇaḥ. Idaṃ kulaputrāḥ tathāgatagarbham ity ucyate.
title: Tathagatagarbha Sutra: Buddha-Nature in All Beings
author: Gautama Buddha (attributed)

en: “Śāriputra, the Thus-Come Ones use only the one Buddha-vehicle to preach the Dharma to living beings. There is no other vehicle, neither a second nor a third. Śāriputra, the Dharma of all the Buddhas of the ten directions is also like this.”
orig: Śāriputra ekaṃ yānam eva tathāgatā buddha-yānaṃ sattvānāṃ dharmaṃ deśayanti. Nāsti dvitīyaṃ vā tṛtīyaṃ vā yānam. Śāriputra daśa-dig-lokānāṃ sarva-buddhānāṃ dharmo ’pi evam eva.
title: Lotus Sutra Chapter 2: All Beings Can Become Buddhas
author: Gautama Buddha (attributed)

en: “Śāriputra, the Buddhas appear in the world solely for the one great purpose: to cause living beings to open the Buddha-knowledge, to show living beings the Buddha-knowledge, to cause living beings to awaken to the Buddha-knowledge, and to cause living beings to enter the path of the Buddha-knowledge.”
orig: Śāriputra ekārtham eva tathāgatā loke utpadyante: sattvānāṃ buddha-jñāna-darśanāya, buddha-jñāna-darśanāvabodhāya, buddha-jñāna-darśanādhigamāya, buddha-jñāna-darśana-mārga-praveśāya.
title: Lotus Sutra Chapter 2: All Beings Can Become Buddhas
author: Gautama Buddha (attributed)

en: “Śāriputra, in the Buddha-lands of the ten directions there is only the one-vehicle Dharma, not two, not three—except where the Buddhas preach by skillful means. It is by means of provisional names and words that they draw living beings to them. They appear in the world to preach the Buddha-wisdom. All beings are my children, and I shall give them all the great vehicle.”
orig: Śāriputra daśasu dig-lokeṣu ekaṃ yāna-dharmaṃ, na dvitīyaṃ na tṛtīyaṃ, anyātra tathāgatānām upāya-deśanāt. Saṃvr̩ti-nāma-pada-vyavaśāt sattvān ākarṣanti. Buddha-jñāna-deśanārtham utpadyante loke. Mama putrāḥ sarva-sattvāḥ, teṣāṃ mahāyānaṃ dāsyāmi.
title: Lotus Sutra Chapter 2: All Beings Can Become Buddhas
author: Gautama Buddha (attributed)

en: “Subhūti, what do you think? Can the Tathāgata be seen by means of bodily characteristics?” “No, World-Honoured One. The Tathāgata cannot be seen by means of bodily characteristics. Why? Because the bodily characteristics spoken of by the Tathāgata are not bodily characteristics.” The Buddha said to Subhūti: “Wherever there are material characteristics there is delusion. If you see all characteristics as non-characteristics, then you see the Tathāgata.”
orig: Tat kiṃ manyase Subhūte lakṣaṇa-saṃpadā tathāgato draṣṭavyaḥ? No hīdaṃ Bhagavan, na lakṣaṇa-saṃpadā tathāgato draṣṭavyaḥ. Tat kasmād dhetoḥ? Yaiṣā Bhagavān lakṣaṇa-saṃpadā bhāṣitā, saivālakṣaṇa-saṃpat. Bhagavān āha: Yāvat Subhūte lakṣaṇa-saṃpadas tāvan mṛṣā. Yadā sarva-lakṣaṇāny alakṣaṇāni paśyasi tadā tathāgataṃ paśyasi.
title: Diamond Sutra: Beyond Form and Non-Form
author: Gautama Buddha (attributed)

en: Subhūti said to the Buddha: “World-Honoured One, will there be living beings in the future who, hearing these words, will have true faith in them?” The Buddha said: “Subhūti, do not speak thus. In the last five hundred years before the final end, there will be those who keep the precepts and cultivate merit, who will be able to give rise to a thought of faith in these words and accept them as truth. Know that such persons have not planted good roots under one Buddha, two Buddhas, three, four, or five Buddhas, but have planted good roots under measureless tens of thousands of Buddhas.”
orig: Āha āyuṣmān Subhūtir Bhagavantam etad avocat: Asti Bhagavan ke cit sattvā bhavisyanti anagata-adhvani pascime kāle pascime samaye pascimāyāṃ pañcaśatyāṃ saddharma-vipralope vartamāne ya imeṣv evaṃrūpeṣu sūtrānta-padeṣu bhāṣyamāṣu bhavat-saṃjñām utpanādāsyānti? Bhagavān āha: Mā Subhūte evaṃ vocaḥ. Bhavisyanti ke cit sattvāḥ śīlavāntaḥ guṇavantaḥ prajñāvantaḥ tatra praśraddadhāsyānti. Na te ekam buddhaṃ dvāv api pañca api buddhān paricaryāṃ kṛtvā, apramāṇaiḥ buddha-śata-sahasraiḥ kuśala-mūlāny avropāyānti.
title: Diamond Sutra: Beyond Form and Non-Form
author: Gautama Buddha (attributed)

en: The Lord said: “Here, Subhūti, a bodhisattva, a great being, having stood in the perfection of wisdom by way of not taking his stand anywhere, should give a gift. He should not stand in form, nor in feeling, nor in perception, nor in mental formations, nor in consciousness. He should give a gift, Subhūti, without standing anywhere, and that is called the perfection of giving.”
orig: Bhagavān āha: Iha Subhūte bodhisattvo mahāsattvaḥ prajñāpāramitāyāṃ sthitvā anutpatti-sthitvā dānaṃ dadāti. Na rūpe tiṣṭhati, na vedanāyāṃ, na saṃjñāyāṃ, na saṃskāreṣu, na vijñāne tiṣṭhati. Dānaṃ dadāti Subhūte kvacid astiṭhitvā, iyaṃ dāna-pāramitā ity ucyate.
title: Prajnaparamita: The Perfection of Wisdom
author: Nagarjuna (attributed)

en: Subhūti said: “World-Honoured One, is the perfection of wisdom a real thing or is it not a real thing?” The Lord said: “It is neither a real thing nor is it not a real thing. Like space, Subhūti, the perfection of wisdom has no distinction, no differentiation. It cannot be seized, cannot be held, cannot be pointed out, cannot be made a sign of, is not something produced, not something stopped.”
orig: Subhūtir āha: Bhagavan kiṃ prajñāpāramitā bhūtā vā abhūtā vā? Bhagavān āha: Na bhūtā nābhūtā. Ākāśavat Subhūte prajñāpāramitā avibhaktā abhedyakā. Na śakyate grahītuṃ, na dhārayituṃ, na nirśdeṣṭuṃ, na nimittīkartuṃ, nābhinirvṛttā, na niruddhā.
title: Prajnaparamita: The Perfection of Wisdom
author: Nagarjuna (attributed)

en: “Subhūti, the bodhisattva who courses in the perfection of wisdom does not see a bodhisattva, does not see the word ‘bodhisattva,’ does not see a perfection of wisdom, does not see the word ‘perfection of wisdom.’ He does not see ‘I course,’ nor ‘I do not course.’ And why? Because the bodhisattva in his very nature is empty, and so is the perfection of wisdom. An empty thing does not course in an empty thing.”
orig: Subhūte bodhisattvo mahāsattvaḥ prajñāpāramitāyāṃ caran na bodhisattvaṃ paśyati, na bodhisattva-śabdaṃ, na prajñāpāramitāṃ, na prajñāpāramitā-śabdaṃ. Na paśyati carāmīti, na na carāmīti. Tat kasmād dhetoḥ? Svabhāvena śūnyā bodhisattvāḥ, śūnyā ca prajñāpāramitā. Śūnyaṃ śūnye na carati.
title: Prajnaparamita: The Perfection of Wisdom
author: Nagarjuna (attributed)

en: Then the Blessed One said: “Mahāmati, the three realms of existence are mind-only. The external world, which is the object of discrimination by the unenlightened, does not exist. What appears to be external is nothing but mind itself, seen through the distortions of habitual patterns. Like a man dreaming, who sees a world that is not there, so the unenlightened see an external reality that is merely their own mind.”
orig: Atha khalu Bhagavān āha: Mahāmate traidhātukaṃ citta-mātram. Bāhyo ’rtho vikalpa-viṣayo bālānāṃ na vidyate. Yad bāhyam iva bhāsate citta-mātram eva tad vāsanā-viparyasta-dṛṣṭam. Yathā svapna-dṛṣṭaḥ puruṣo ’vidyamānaṃ jagat paśyati, evam bālā bāhyam arthaṃ paśyanti sva-citta-mātram.
title: Lankavatara Sutra: Mind-Only
author: Gautama Buddha (attributed)

en: “Mahāmati, the store-consciousness (ālaya-vijñāna) is like the ocean, and the seven active consciousnesses are like its waves. When the wind of objects stirs the ocean of the store-consciousness, the waves of the seven consciousnesses arise ceaselessly. But when the discriminating mind is stilled and the habitual patterns are exhausted, the store-consciousness undergoes a revolution at its base (āśraya-parāvr̩tti), and what was defiled consciousness becomes undefiled wisdom.”
orig: Mahāmate ālaya-vijñānaṃ sāgaravat, sapta-vijñānāni tarangavat. Viṣaya-vāyunā ālaya-sāgare ksobhite sapta-vijñāna-tarangaḥ satātam utpadyante. Yadā tu vikalpo nivṛtto vāsanāś ca kṣīṇāḥ, tadā āśraya-parāvr̩ttiḥ saṃpadyate, saṃkliṣṭaṃ vijñānaṃ viśuddhaṃ jñānaṃ bhavati.
title: Lankavatara Sutra: Mind-Only
author: Gautama Buddha (attributed)

en: “Mahāmati, the truth realized by the Tathāgatas is neither words nor non-words. Words are not truth, yet truth is not apart from words. It is like a man pointing at the moon with his finger: the foolish look at the finger, not the moon. So those who cling to the letter do not understand the truth that my words are intended to convey.”
orig: Mahāmate tathāgatair adhigataṃ tattvaṃ na vāk nāvāk. Vāg na tattvaṃ, na ca tattvaṃ vāg-vyatiriktaṃ. Yathā kaścit puruṣaḥ candram aṅgulyagreṇa nirśdisati, bālās tv aṅgulyagram eva paśyanti na candraṃ. Evaṃ ye akṣareṣu sajjante na tattvaṃ jānanti yan mama vākyaṃ sandhatte.
title: Lankavatara Sutra: Mind-Only
author: Gautama Buddha (attributed)

en: The Patriarch said: “Good friends, the self-nature of human beings is originally pure. The ten thousand dharmas are all within the self-nature. If one thinks of all the evil things, then evil actions are performed. If one thinks of all the good things, then good actions are performed. Thus all dharmas are within the self-nature. The self-nature is always pure, just as the sun and moon are always bright. It is only because of clouds that there is darkness above and brightness below. When a sudden wind blows the clouds away, all is bright, and the ten thousand forms appear.”
orig: Shī yún: Shànzhī, rénxìng běnlái qīngjìng. Wànfǎ jiē zài zìxìng. Ruò sī yīqiē èshì, jí xíng èxíng. Ruò sī yīqiē shànshì, jí xíng shànxíng. Shì zhī wànfǎ jiē zài zìxìng. Zìxìng chángqīng, rú rì yuè chángmíng. Zhǐ wèi yúngài, shàngmíng xiààn. Hūrán fēngchuī yúnsàn, shàngxià jùmíng, wànxìng jiē xiàn.
title: Platform Sutra: Your Nature Is Originally Pure
author: Huineng

en: Huineng composed this verse: “Bodhi originally has no tree. The bright mirror also has no stand. Originally there is not a single thing—where can dust alight?”
orig: Huìnéng yǒu jì yīn: Pútí běn wú shù, míngjìng yì fēi tái. Běnlái wú yī wù, héchù rě chénāi?
title: Platform Sutra: Your Nature Is Originally Pure
author: Huineng

en: The Patriarch said: “Good friends, in this teaching of mine, from ancient times up to the present, all have set up no-thought as the main doctrine, non-form as the substance, and non-abiding as the root. What is non-form? Non-form is to be apart from form while in the midst of forms. No-thought is to be without thought while in the midst of thoughts. Non-abiding is the original nature of human beings.”
orig: Shī yún: Shànzhī, wǒ cǐ fǎmén, cóng shàngyǐlái, xiānjīn jīn dài, jiē lì wúnàn wéizōng, wúxìang wéitǐ, wúzhù wéiběn. Wúxìang zhě hé? Yú xiàngér lí xiàng. Wúnàn zhě hé? Yú niànér wú niàn. Wúzhù zhě, rén zhī běnxìng.
title: Platform Sutra: Your Nature Is Originally Pure
author: Huineng

en: A monk asked Zhaozhou in all earnestness: “Does a dog have Buddha-nature or not?” Zhaozhou said: “Mu.”
orig: Zhàozhōu yīn sēng wèn: Gǒu zǐ hái yǒu fóxìng yě wú? Zhōu yún: Wú.
title: Joshu's Mu: Does a Dog Have Buddha-Nature?
author: Wumen Huikai

en: For the practice of Zen, you must pass through the barrier of the patriarchs. To attain wondrous awakening, you must completely cut off the mind-road. If you do not pass through the barrier and do not cut off the mind-road, then you are like a ghost clinging to grasses and trees. What is the barrier of the patriarchs? Simply this single word “Mu”—that is the front gate of Zen. Therefore it is called the “Gateless Barrier of the Zen School.” If you pass through it, you will not only see Zhaozhou face to face, but you will walk hand in hand with the whole line of patriarchs. You will see with the same eyes they see with and hear with the same ears. Is that not a joyful thing?
orig: Cān Chán xū tòu zǔshī guān, miàowù yāo qióng xīnlù jué. Zǔguān bú tòu, xīnlù bú jué, jìn rú yī cǎò mù zhī guǐ. Rúhé shì zǔshī guān? Zhǐ zhè yī gè Wú zì, nǎ shì Zōng mén yī guān. Suǒyǐ huàn zànchéng Chánzōng Wúménguān. Ruò tòudé guò, fēi dàn qīnjìàn Zhàozhōu, biàn yǔ lìdài zǔshī bǎ shǒu gòngxíng, méimaorú, tóngyī yǎn jìng jiàn, tóngyī ěr wén. Qǐ búqunǐzāi!
title: Joshu's Mu: Does a Dog Have Buddha-Nature?
author: Wumen Huikai

en: The dog, the Buddha-nature—the whole thing is presented. A moment’s hesitation and you miss it. If you chase after words and pursue phrases, you are a dead man.
orig: Gǒu zǐ fóxìng, quántí lìnglù. Cái shè yǒu wú, sàngshēn shīmìng. Ruò zhúcí zī jú, jí sǐ rén yě.
title: Joshu's Mu: Does a Dog Have Buddha-Nature?
author: Wumen Huikai

en: Then King Milinda said to the Venerable Nāgasena: “How is your reverence known? What is your name, venerable sir?” “I am known as Nāgasena, O King, and it is by that name that my fellow monks address me. But although parents give such a name as Nāgasena, it is nevertheless just a name, a designation, a conceptual term, a conventional usage. There is no permanent individual (puggala) to be found therein.”
orig: Atha kho rājā Milindo āyasmantaṃ Nāgasenaṃ etad avoca: Kena bho tvaṃ ñāyasi? Ko nāma tvaṃ bhante? Nāgaseno ahaṃ mahārāja ñāyāmi, Nāgasenoti maṃ sabrahmacarino samājapenti. Api ca kho mahārāja mātāpitaro nāmaṃ karonti Nāgasenoti vā; tathāpi kho mahārāja saṅkhā samannā paññatti voharamattaṃ Nāgasenoti, na hettha puggalo upalabbhati.
title: Milindapanha: The Chariot Analogy
author: Nagasena (attributed)

en: Then Nāgasena said to King Milinda: “Your Majesty, how did you come here—on foot or in a vehicle?” “I came in a chariot, venerable sir.” “If you came in a chariot, explain to me what the chariot is. Is the pole the chariot?” “No, venerable sir.” “Is the axle the chariot? Are the wheels the chariot? Is the frame the chariot? Is the yoke the chariot?” “No, venerable sir.” “Is the chariot all of these parts taken together?” “No, venerable sir.” “Is the chariot something apart from these parts?” “No, venerable sir.” “Then, Your Majesty, I can discover no chariot. The chariot is just a sound. What then is the chariot? Your Majesty has spoken a falsehood—there is no chariot!”
orig: Atha kho āyasmā Nāgaseno rājānaṃ Milindaṃ etad avoca: Katham pana tvaṃ mahārāja idhagato? Pattiyā udha yānena? Rathenahaṃ bhante āgato. Yadi tvaṃ mahārāja rathena āgato, ācikkha me rathaṃ. Kiṃ īsā ratho? Na hidaṃ bhante. Kiṃ akkho ratho? Kiṃ cakkāni ratho? Kiṃ rathapāñjā ratho? Kiṃ yugaddaṇḍaṃ ratho? Na hidaṃ bhante. Kiṃ pana mahārāja ete sabbe ratho? Na hidaṃ bhante. Kiṃ pana mahārāja aññatra etehi ratho? Na hidaṃ bhante. Na khvāhaṃ mahārāja rathaṃ paśyāmi. Saddamattaṃ hi esa mahārāja ratho. Ko pan’ettha ratho? Musā tvaṃ mahārāja bhaṇasi, natthi ratho!
title: Milindapanha: The Chariot Analogy
author: Nagasena (attributed)

en: King Milinda said: “I have not spoken a falsehood, venerable Nāgasena. It is on account of the pole, the axle, the wheels, the frame, and the yoke that ‘chariot’ exists as a designation, a conceptual term, a conventional usage, a name.” “Excellent, Your Majesty! You understand the chariot. In exactly the same way, it is on account of hair, the body, feelings, perceptions, mental formations, and consciousness that ‘Nāgasena’ exists as a designation. But in the ultimate sense, no person is to be found therein. As the nun Vajirā said in the presence of the Blessed One: ‘Just as the word chariot is used when the parts are assembled, so the conventional term being is used when the aggregates are present.’”
orig: Na khvāhaṃ bhante Nāgasena musā bhaṇāmi. Īsāñ ca paṭicca, akkhañ ca paṭicca, cakkāni ca paṭicca, rathapāñjañ ca paṭicca, yugaddaṇḍañ ca paṭicca, ratho ti saṅkhā samannā paññatti voharamattaṃ nāmamattaṃ. Sādhu mahārāja! Rathapassi tvaṃ. Evam eva kho mahārāja kesādiṃ paṭicca, rūpaṃ paṭicca, vedanāṃ paṭicca, saññaṃ paṭicca, saṅkhāre paṭicca, viññāṇaṃ paṭicca, Nāgasenoti saṅkhā samannā paññatti voharamattaṃ. Paramatthato pan’ettha puggalo na upalabbhati. Yathā hi aṅgasambārā hoti saddo ratho iti, evam khandhesu santesu hoti satto ti sammāti.
title: Milindapanha: The Chariot Analogy
author: Nagasena (attributed)

en: Once, when I had begun to think about the things that are, and my thoughts had soared exceedingly high, while my bodily senses were restrained as in sleep—though not such sleep as comes from heaviness of food or weariness of body—I thought a being of vast and boundless magnitude came toward me, called my name, and said to me: “What do you wish to hear and see, and having seen, to learn and know?”
orig: Ἐννοίας μοί ποτε γενομένης περὶ τῶν Ὄντων, καὶ μετεωρισθείσης μου τῆς διανοίας σφόδρα, κατασχεθεισῶν μου τῶν σωματικῶν αἰσθήσεων
title: Corpus Hermeticum I: Poimandres
author: Hermes Trismegistus (attributed)

en: I said: “Who are you?” He replied: “I am Poimandres, the Mind of the Sovereignty. I know what you wish, and I am with you everywhere.”
orig: τίς εἶ σύ; φησίν. Ἐγὼ μέν εἰμι ὁ Ποιμάνδρης, ὁ τῆς αὐθεντίας νοῦς.
title: Corpus Hermeticum I: Poimandres
author: Hermes Trismegistus (attributed)

en: I said: “I wish to learn about the things that are, and understand their nature, and know God.” He said: “Hold in your mind all that you wish to learn, and I will teach you.” With these words, he changed his form, and suddenly everything was opened before me in a moment, and I saw a boundless vision: all became light, serene and joyful. And I fell in love with it.
orig: βούλομαι μαθεῖν τὰ Ὄντα καὶ νοῆσαι τὴν φύσιν αὐτῶν καὶ γνῶναι τὸν θεόν.
title: Corpus Hermeticum I: Poimandres
author: Hermes Trismegistus (attributed)

en: That light is I, Mind, your God, who was before the watery nature that appeared out of the darkness. And the luminous Word that issued from Mind is the Son of God.
orig: τὸ φῶς ἐκεῖνο ἐγώ, νοῦς, ὁ σὸς θεός, ὁ πρὸ τῆς ἐκ σκότους φανείσης ὑγρᾶς φύσεως.
title: Corpus Hermeticum I: Poimandres
author: Hermes Trismegistus (attributed)

en: Now Mind, the Father of all, being life and light, brought forth Man, equal to himself, and loved him as his own child. For Man was very beautiful, bearing the image of his Father. For indeed God fell in love with his own form, and gave over to him all his works. And Man, seeing the creation which the Demiurge had fashioned in the fire, wished also to make things, and the Father gave him permission.
orig: ὁ δὲ Νοῦς ὁ πατὴρ πάντων, ὤν ζωὴ καὶ φῶς, ἀπεκύησεν ἄνθρωπον αὐτῷ ἴσον
title: Corpus Hermeticum I: Poimandres
author: Hermes Trismegistus (attributed)

en: And Man, who had full power over the world of mortal creatures and of animals without reason, stooped through the harmony of the spheres, having broken through their envelopes, and showed to the downward-tending Nature the beautiful form of God. When Nature saw him who had in himself the inexhaustible beauty and all the energy of the Governors, combined with the form of God, she smiled with love.
orig: ὁ δὲ πᾶσαν ἐξουσίαν ἔχων τοῦ τῶν θνητῶν κόσμου
title: Corpus Hermeticum I: Poimandres
author: Hermes Trismegistus (attributed)

en: “But first you must tear off this garment which you wear—this cloak of darkness, this web of ignorance, this prop of evil, this bond of corruption, this living death, this conscious corpse, this tomb you carry around with you, this robber in your house, who hates you through what he loves, and envies you through what he hates.”
orig: πρῶτον μὲν ἐν τῇ ἀναλύσει τοῦ σώματος ὑλικοῦ παραδίδως αὐτὸ τὸ σῶμα
title: Corpus Hermeticum I: Poimandres
author: Hermes Trismegistus (attributed)

en: And then the soul, stripped of all that the spheres had wrought upon it, enters the eighth sphere, having only its own proper power, and together with those already there, it hymns the Father. And those present rejoice at its coming, and becoming like its companions, it hears certain powers above the eighth sphere hymning God with a sweet voice. And they ascend in order to the Father, and surrender themselves to the powers, and becoming powers themselves, enter into God. This is the good end of those who have gained knowledge: to become God.
orig: καὶ τότε γυμνωθεῖσα ἡ ψυχὴ ἀπὸ τῶν τῆς ἁρμονίας ἐνεργημάτων... τοῦτο ἐστι τὸ ἀγαθὸν τέλος τοῖς γνῶσιν ἐσχηκόσι, θεωθῆναι.
title: Corpus Hermeticum I: Poimandres
author: Hermes Trismegistus (attributed)

en: Since the Maker made the whole cosmos not with hands but by the Word, you should conceive of him as present and as always existing, as having made all things, as one alone, and as having fashioned by his own will the things that are. For that is his body: neither tangible, nor visible, nor measurable, nor dimensional, nor like any other body. It is not fire, nor water, nor air, nor breath, but all things were made from it.
orig: ἐπειδὴ τὸν κόσμον ὅλον ἐποίησεν ὁ δημιουργὸς οὐ χερσίν, ἀλλὰ λόγῳ
title: Corpus Hermeticum IV: The Crater of Nous
author: Hermes Trismegistus (attributed)

en: Having willed to constitute the human race, God filled a great mixing bowl (krater, κρατήρ) with Mind (Nous) and sent it down, appointing a herald and commanding him to proclaim to the hearts of men: “Baptize yourself in this bowl, you who are able, you who believe that you shall ascend to him who sent the bowl down, you who know for what purpose you were made.”
orig: πληρώσας κρατῆρα μέγαν τούτου καταπέμψας, δοὺς κήρυκα καὶ ἐκέλευσε κηρύξαι ταῖς τῶν ἀνθρώπων καρδίαις τάδε· βάπτισον σεαυτὴν τῷ κρατῆρι τούτῳ
title: Corpus Hermeticum IV: The Crater of Nous
author: Hermes Trismegistus (attributed)

en: Those who heeded the proclamation and were baptized in Mind, these partook of gnosis and became perfect men, having received Mind. But those who missed the proclamation, these have reason (logos) but not Mind (nous), not knowing why they were made or by whom.
orig: οἱ μὲν οὖν συνέντες τοῦ κηρύγματος καὶ βαπτισάμενοι τοῦ νοός, οὗτοι μετέσχον τῆς γνώσεως
title: Corpus Hermeticum IV: The Crater of Nous
author: Hermes Trismegistus (attributed)

en: “But why, Father, did God not distribute Mind to all?” “Because he wished, my child, that Mind should be set in the midst as a prize for souls to win.”
orig: διὰ τί, ὦ πάτερ, οὐκ ἐμέρισεν πᾶσι τὸν νοῦν; ἐβουλήθη, ὦ τέκνον, τοῦτον ἐν μέσῳ ταῖς ψυχαῖς ἆθλον ἱδρῦσθαι.
title: Corpus Hermeticum IV: The Crater of Nous
author: Hermes Trismegistus (attributed)

en: For the one who has received Mind—whither does he hasten? To the light. Where does he wish to go? To the Father. What does he desire? To be freed from evil. And this cannot happen to one who has not received Mind. Therefore the one who has knowledge is good and pious and already divine.
orig: ὁ γὰρ μετασχὼν τοῦ νοὸς... ἤδη θεῖός ἐστι.
title: Corpus Hermeticum IV: The Crater of Nous
author: Hermes Trismegistus (attributed)

en: Yesterday, Asclepius, I delivered the perfect discourse. Now I think it necessary to follow it up by discussing the nature of sensation. Sensation and thought differ in this: sensation belongs to matter, thought to essence. But to me, thought does not differ from sensation, at least in humans; for thought is sensible and sensation is thoughtful. In God, however, thought and sensation are one.
orig: χθὲς, ὦ Ἀσκληπιέ, τὸν τέλειον λόγον ἀπέδωκα
title: Corpus Hermeticum XI: All Things in God
author: Hermes Trismegistus (attributed)

en: If you do not make yourself equal to God, you cannot understand God; for like is known by like. Grow to immeasurable size. Be free from every body, transcend all time. Become eternity and thus you will understand God. Believe that nothing is impossible for you. Consider yourself immortal and able to understand everything: all arts, all sciences, the nature of every living creature.
orig: ἐὰν μὴ σεαυτὸν ἐξισώσῃς τῷ θεῷ, τὸν θεὸν νοῆσαι οὐ δύνασαι· τὸ γὰρ ὅμοιον τῷ ὁμοίῳ νοεῖται.
title: Corpus Hermeticum XI: All Things in God
author: Hermes Trismegistus (attributed)

en: Look at what I now show you. There are many forms of every kind of living thing. Can you see where they all are? In God. And where is God? Not in heaven above, nor below the earth, nor in any other place. He is in all these things. For all things are in God, and God is in all things. God is not separate from any being.
orig: πάντα γὰρ ἐν τῷ θεῷ, καὶ ὁ θεὸς ἐν πᾶσιν. οὐδενὸς ἀπολείπεται ὁ θεός.
title: Corpus Hermeticum XI: All Things in God
author: Hermes Trismegistus (attributed)

en: God is not Mind, but the cause of Mind; not Spirit, but the cause of Spirit; not Light, but the cause of Light. We must honour God by these two names, which belong to him alone and to no other. For none of the other beings called gods, none of men or demons, can be even slightly good apart from God alone.
orig: οὐ νοῦς ἐστιν ὁ θεός, αἴτιος δὲ τοῦ εἶναι νοῦν
title: Corpus Hermeticum XI: All Things in God
author: Hermes Trismegistus (attributed)

en: Such is the beautiful image of God, if indeed you can contemplate it. But you cannot contemplate it with these eyes of yours. The image of God cannot be seen with hands or feet, but with mind and heart. If you can contemplate this, then believe me: you will find the path of ascent. Or rather, the image itself will lead you on the way.
orig: τοιαύτη ἡ καλὴ εἰκὼν τοῦ θεοῦ
title: Corpus Hermeticum XI: All Things in God
author: Hermes Trismegistus (attributed)

en: Tat said: “In the General Discourses, Father, you spoke in riddles and did not reveal yourself when you spoke of the divine. You said that no one can be saved before the rebirth. When I became your suppliant, after you had spoken with me on the mountain, I asked to learn the discourse on rebirth, since of all discourses this is the only one I do not know. You said you would give it to me when I was ready to become a stranger to the world.”
orig: Ἐν τοῖς Γενικοῖς, ὦ πάτερ, αἰνιγματωδῶς καὶ οὐκ ἐπιφανῶς διελέξω περὶ θειότητος
title: Corpus Hermeticum XIII: On Rebirth
author: Hermes Trismegistus (attributed)

en: Hermes said: “The one who is born of this rebirth, O son, is a different being: a god, a child of God, the all in all, composed of all the powers.” Tat said: “You are driving me mad, Father. What you say makes no sense. I wish to ask you more plainly: Am I then a stranger to my father’s race?”
orig: ὁ γεννηθεὶς ἐκ ταύτης τῆς γεννήσεως, ὦ τέκνον, ἄλλος ἔσται· θεός, θεοῦ παῖς
title: Corpus Hermeticum XIII: On Rebirth
author: Hermes Trismegistus (attributed)

en: The tent of the body we have passed through, O son. We have been constituted in the Ogdoad. The torments flee away. Let us now receive the powers. Knowledge of God has come to us, and with it knowledge has come: joy. With joy comes self-control. With self-control comes endurance. The tenth power that drives out the twelve: truth.
orig: τὴν σκηνὴν παρήλθομεν, ὦ τέκνον. ἐν τῇ ὀγδοάδι συνεστάθημεν.
title: Corpus Hermeticum XIII: On Rebirth
author: Hermes Trismegistus (attributed)

en: “I am in heaven, in earth, in water, in air. I am in animals, in plants. I am in the womb, before the womb, after the womb. I am everywhere.”
orig: ἐγώ εἰμι ἐν οὐρανῷ, ἐν γῇ, ἐν ὕδατι, ἐν ἀέρι. ἐν ζώοις, ἐν φυτοῖς. ἐν γαστρί, πρὸ γαστρός, μετὰ γαστρός. πανταχοῦ.
title: Corpus Hermeticum XIII: On Rebirth
author: Hermes Trismegistus (attributed)

en: From now on, O son, since the mind in us has been purified by the powers, the vision of the divine has come. For the rebirth is not something that can be taught, nor can it be seen through this material element. The old composite form has been dissolved. I am no longer what I was. I have been born in Mind.
orig: τὸ λοιπόν, ὦ τέκνον, καθαρεύσαντος ἐν ἡμῖν τοῦ νοῦ ταῖς δυνάμεσιν
title: Corpus Hermeticum XIII: On Rebirth
author: Hermes Trismegistus (attributed)

en: A great miracle, Asclepius, is man. An honour-worthy and wonder-worthy living being. For he passes into the nature of a god as though he himself were a god. He knows the race of demons, inasmuch as he recognizes that he shares the same origin with them. He despises that part of his nature which is merely human, trusting in the divinity of his other part.
orig: Magnum miraculum est homo, o Asclepi. Animal honorandum atque adorandum. Hic enim in naturam dei transit quasi ipse sit deus. Hic daemonum genus novit, utpote qui cum isdem ortum se esse cognoscat. Hic humanae naturae partem in se ipse despicit, alterius partis divinitate confisus.
title: Asclepius 6: Man Is a Great Miracle
author: Hermes Trismegistus (attributed)

en: How much happier is the blend of human nature! Joined to the gods by a kindred divinity, he despises inwardly that part of himself by which he is earthly. All other living creatures he binds to himself in a knot of love, knowing himself to be related not only to the gods above but also to the natural world below. He cultivates the earth, he mixes himself with the elements by the swiftness of thought, he descends to the depths of the sea by the keenness of his mind.
orig: O quam felicior est hominis mixtura naturae! Diis cognata divinitate coniunctus est, partem sui qua terrenus est intra se ipse despiciens. Ceterorum omnium animantium necessitudinis sibi nexu devincit, terram colit, elementis celeritate sensus admiscetur, acumine mentis profundo maris descendit.
title: Asclepius 6: Man Is a Great Miracle
author: Hermes Trismegistus (attributed)

en: Everything is permitted to him. Heaven itself does not seem too high, for he measures it as though it were nearby by the keenness of his thought. No mist of the air dims the concentration of his mind. The density of the earth does not impede his work. The depth of the waters does not blunt his downcast gaze. He is at once all things, and he is at once everywhere.
orig: Omnia illi licent. Caelum non putat excelsum, quasi de proximo metitur illud acumine sensus. Nulla aeris caligo intentionem animi eius obcaecat. Non densitas terrae eius operi obstat. Non profunditas aquae prospectum eius obtundit demerso. Omnia idem est et ubique idem est.
title: Asclepius 6: Man Is a Great Miracle
author: Hermes Trismegistus (attributed)

en: What then must we do? What device can we employ? How can one see the “inconceivable beauty” which stays within the holy sanctuary and does not come out where the profane may see it? Let whoever can, follow and come within, and leave outside the sight of his eyes and not turn back to the former splendours of bodies.
orig: τί οὖν χρὴ ποιεῖν; τί μηχανᾶσθαι; πῶς ἄν τις θεάσαιτο κάλλος ἀμήχανον
title: Enneads I.6.9: Cut Away Everything
author: Plotinus

en: For when one sees the beauties of body, one must not run toward them but know that they are images, traces, shadows—and flee toward that of which they are likenesses. For if someone should pursue them, wishing to grasp them as real, he will be like the one who wished to grasp the beautiful image floating on the water—as a certain myth, I think, signified—and sinking into the deep current, disappeared. In the same way, the one who clings to beautiful bodies and does not let go will sink, not in body but in soul, into the dark depths, hateful to the intellect, where, blind, he will dwell in Hades, consorting with shadows there too.
orig: ὃς γὰρ ἂν διώκῃ τὰ σωμάτων καλά... καταδὺς εἰς τὸν βυθὸν ἀφανὴς γένηται
title: Enneads I.6.9: Cut Away Everything
author: Plotinus

en: “Let us flee then to our dear fatherland”—this is the truest counsel. But what is this flight? And how do we escape? We shall set sail, as Odysseus did from Circe or Calypso—as the poet says, I think, with a hidden meaning—and was not content to stay though he had delights of the eyes and much sensible beauty to enjoy. Our fatherland is where we came from, and our Father is there.
orig: φεύγωμεν δὴ φίλην ἐς πατρίδα... πατρὶς δὲ ἡμῖν ὅθεν ἤλθομεν καὶ πατὴρ ἐκεῖ.
title: Enneads I.6.9: Cut Away Everything
author: Plotinus

en: What then is our means of flight? Not by feet—for feet carry us always from land to land. Nor must you prepare a chariot drawn by horses or a ship. You must cut away all this and not look, but close your eyes and exchange this way of seeing for another, and awaken a faculty that everyone possesses but few use. And what does this inner sight see? It cannot, when just awakened, look at the brilliance before it. The soul must be trained first to look at beautiful practices, then beautiful works—not those which the arts produce, but those of men who are called good. Then look at the soul of those who produce the beautiful works.
orig: τίς οὖν ἡ φυγή; οὐ ποσὶν... περιελεῖν πάντα δεῖ
title: Enneads I.6.9: Cut Away Everything
author: Plotinus

en: Go back into yourself and look. If you do not yet see yourself as beautiful, then, just as the sculptor of a statue that must be beautiful cuts away here and polishes there, makes this line lighter and that one purer until he has given the statue a beautiful face—so you too must cut away what is excessive, straighten what is crooked, purify what is dark to make it bright, and never stop “working on your statue” until the divine splendour of virtue shines in you, until you see “self-mastery enthroned upon its holy seat.”
orig: ἄναγε ἐπὶ σαυτὸν καὶ ἴδε· κἂν μήπω σεαυτὸν ἴδῃς καλόν, οἷα ἀγαλματοποιός...
title: Enneads I.6.9: Cut Away Everything
author: Plotinus

en: If you have become this, and seen it, and been with yourself in purity, with nothing hindering you from becoming one in this way, with nothing else mixed in with yourself, but being entirely yourself alone, true light—not measured by size, not bounded by shape into smallness, not expanded to size by unboundedness, but everywhere unmeasured, because greater than all measure and more than all quantity—if you see that you have become this, then you have become sight itself. Trust yourself then. You have already ascended and need no one to show you. Concentrate your gaze and see. This alone is the eye that sees the great beauty.
orig: εἰ τοίνυν γέγονας τοῦτο... θαρρήσας σεαυτῷ. ἤδη ἀναβέβηκας.
title: Enneads I.6.9: Cut Away Everything
author: Plotinus

en: Since there were not two things but the seer himself was one with the seen—for it was not really seen but united to him—if he should remember what he was when he merged with the One, he would have an image of the One in himself. He himself was one, having no distinction in himself either in relation to himself or to other things. For there was no movement in him, no passion, no desire for another thing was present in him after the ascent. Not even reason, not even thought was present. He himself was not there, if we must say even this. But as if caught away or possessed by a god, he had passed in stillness into a state of solitude, his being at rest and not inclining to any side.
orig: ἐπεὶ οὐ δύο ἦν, ἀλλὰ ἓν ἦν αὐτὸς ὁ ἰδὼν πρὸς τὸ ὑφ’ αὑτοῦ ὁρώμενον
title: Enneads VI.9.11: The Flight of the Alone to the Alone
author: Plotinus

en: He did not turn to anything at all, not even to himself. He was altogether at rest and had become a kind of stillness. He did not concern himself even with the beautiful, but had already gone beyond beauty and gone beyond the choir of the virtues. He was like someone who, entering the inner sanctuary, has left the statues in the temple behind him—they will be the first things he sees when he comes out of the sanctuary, after the vision within, after communion not with a statue or an image but with the divine itself.
orig: οὐδὲ πρὸς αὑτόν· πάντη ἀτρεμὴς καὶ οἷον στάσις γενόμενος
title: Enneads VI.9.11: The Flight of the Alone to the Alone
author: Plotinus

en: Perhaps one should not speak of “seeing,” but rather of a different kind of seeing, an ecstasy and simplification and self-surrender, a reaching out toward contact and a stillness and a meditation directed toward fitting oneself to what is to be seen in the sanctuary. But anyone who tries to see in any other way will find nothing there. These then are but images, and the wise among the prophets expressed in riddles how God is seen. But a wise priest, understanding the riddle, may make the true vision of the sanctuary, going within.
orig: οὐκ ἂν οὖν οὐδὲ ὅρασις λέγοιτο, ἀλλὰ ἄλλος τρόπος τοῦ ἰδεῖν, ἔκστασις καὶ ἁπλωσις καὶ ἐπίδοσις αὑτοῦ
title: Enneads VI.9.11: The Flight of the Alone to the Alone
author: Plotinus

en: This is the life of gods and of godlike and blessed men: liberation from all things here below, a life that takes no delight in the things of earth, the flight of the alone to the Alone.
orig: αὕτη ζωὴ θεῶν καὶ θείων καὶ εὐδαιμόνων ἀνθρώπων βίος· ἀπαλλαγὴ τῶν ἄλλων τῶν τῇδε... φυγὴ μόνου πρὸς μόνον.
title: Enneads VI.9.11: The Flight of the Alone to the Alone
author: Plotinus

en: How then does Intellect come from the One? Since the One is perfect, seeking nothing, having nothing, needing nothing, it overflowed, as it were, and its superabundance made something other than itself. That which was generated turned back to the One and was filled, and looking toward it became Intellect. Its halt and turning toward the One constituted being; its gaze upon the One constituted Intellect.
orig: πῶς οὖν ἐκ τοῦ ἑνὸς νοῦς; ὃτι τέλειον τὸ ἓν, τῷ μηδὲν ζητεῖν μηδὲ ἔχειν μηδὲ δεῖσθαι, οἷον ὑπερερρύη
title: Enneads V.1.6: The Emanation of the One
author: Plotinus

en: Since it stood in relation to the One and gazed upon it, it became at once both Intellect and Being. Standing in the place of the One, it necessarily produces things similar, pouring out a great power. And this too was a form of that which poured it out, as the One was before it. This activity from the substance of Intellect is Soul, which came to be while Intellect stayed in its place—just as Intellect came to be while the One stayed in its place. But Soul does not stay in its place; it produces an image of itself, which is the sense world.
orig: στᾶσα γὰρ πρὸς αὐτὸ καὶ πρὸς αὐτὸ ἰδοῦσα νοῦς ἅμα καὶ ὂν ἐγένετο
title: Enneads V.1.6: The Emanation of the One
author: Plotinus

en: Each thing that has reached its perfection produces something. That which is eternally perfect produces eternally, and what it produces is eternally less than itself. What then must we say about the Most Perfect? That nothing comes from it except that which is greatest after it. That which is greatest after it is Intellect, and Intellect is second. For Intellect sees the One, and needs only the One. But the One has no need of Intellect. And what is produced by that which is above Intellect must be Intellect. And Intellect is the greatest of all things after the One, because all other things are below it.
orig: ἕκαστον τὸ εἰς τελειότητα ἐλθὸν γεννᾷ
title: Enneads V.1.6: The Emanation of the One
author: Plotinus

en: Every manifold in some way participates in unity. For if it in no way participated in unity, neither would the whole be one whole, nor would each of the many of which the manifold consists be one—for each of them would itself be a manifold, and this to infinity. And each of these infinites would itself be infinitely infinite. For a manifold that participates in unity in no way, neither as a whole nor in respect of each of its parts, would be infinite in every respect and in respect of every one of its parts. For each of the many, whatever one you take, will be either one or not-one; and if not-one, then either many or nothing. But if each is nothing, the whole composed of nothings is nothing. And if each is many, each will be composed of infinitely many infinities. But this is impossible. Therefore every manifold participates in unity.
orig: Πᾶν πλῆθος μετέχει πῃ τοῦ ἑνός.
title: Elements of Theology 1-3: Every Manifold Participates in Unity
author: Proclus

en: All that participates in unity is both one and not-one. For if it is not the One itself—since it participates in unity by having unity, not by being unity—it has acquired unity by participation and therefore has been affected in respect of its unity-having. But what has been affected in respect of its participation in unity, insofar as it has been affected, is not-one. And since it is not-one but has come to participate in unity, it is at once both one and not-one. It is not one in the same respect in which it is not-one, but is one by participation and not-one in its own nature.
orig: Πᾶν τὸ τοῦ ἑνὸς μετέχον καὶ ἕν ἐστι καὶ οὐχ ἕν.
title: Elements of Theology 1-3: Every Manifold Participates in Unity
author: Proclus

en: All that becomes one does so by participating in the One, and derives its unity from the One. For if what is not essentially one is nevertheless one, it has surely become one by having been affected in a certain way and by having acquired unity. And to acquire unity is to participate in unity. Therefore, if it is one insofar as it participates, and what participates derives what it participates in from its source, then the unity of what is unified derives from the One, which is the first principle of unity.
orig: Πᾶν τὸ γινόμενον ἓν τῇ τοῦ ἑνὸς μεθέξει ἓν γίνεται.
title: Elements of Theology 1-3: Every Manifold Participates in Unity
author: Proclus
